By combining AI-driven summarization with multi-source verification, the proposed system enhances digital literacy, promotes critical thinking, and supports informed decision-making. Overall, this system represents a significant advancement toward building a reliable and transparent information ecosystem, where users can access concise, accurate, and trustworthy information in real time. The proposed approach not only addresses current challenges in misinformation detection but also provides a scalable foundation for future enhancements in intelligent information processing systems.
Keywords: Misinformation; AI; NLP; Summarization; Authenticity Verification

title: Combating Digital Deception: A Taxonomical and Systematic Review of Deepfake Detection Approaches
authors: Pratishtha Gupta; Bhawna Narwal; Amar Kumar Mohapatra
venue: WIREs Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; News Media; Forums & Communities
Tags: Deepfake; Machine Learning; Cybersecurity; Detection Techniques
abstract: ABSTRACT The contemporary world is fuelled by the social web, with approximately 504.17 h of video content and 1,050,000 photos going up online every minute. This open‐access media is swiftly exploited to facilitate deepfake generation that can disseminate misleading information and tarnish reputations. Furthermore, the subtlety of deepfakes has escalated to the point where deep learning‐based techniques yield deepfakes that are perceptibly flawless and indistinguishable from reality. Moreover, the existing detection models suffer severe affliction when in the crosshairs of adversarial attacks. Therefore, it is paramount to promote the development of resilient evasion strategies against this societal bane, which “takes the bull by the horns” while shielding them from hostile adversaries. In this direction, the presented article outlines the neoteric progression in deepfake topography, assimilating generation, detection, and adversarial attack‐based techniques. It delineates an all‐inclusive taxonomy based on topical research stemming from the deepfake landscape. Based on the taxonomical classification, a systematic and exhaustive review of the avant‐garde deepfake detection techniques is conducted. Furthermore, this article reviews state‐of‐the‐art active defense techniques against audio/video deepfakes, incorporating both generator‐ and detector‐based adversarial attacks, and discusses issues with deepfake datasets. Lastly, the trends in deepfake detection and dataset utilization have been highlighted, bringing to the fore the all‐important spheres requiring futuristic investigation. This article is categorized under: Technologies > Artificial Intelligence Technologies > Machine Learning Commercial, Legal, and Ethical Issues > Security and Privacy
Keywords: Deepfake Detection; Digital Deception; AI; Adversarial Attacks; Information Verification
affiliations: Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women

title: Ambient Journalism after Twitter
authors: Alfred Hermida
Tags: media studies; social media; algorithmic influence; information overload; synthetic ambience
abstract: The concept of ambient journalism emerged to describe how digital platforms, particularly Twitter, enabled wide and incidental news consumption. I defined it as a broad, asynchronous, lightweight, and always-on communication system that enabled citizens to maintain a “mental model” of news, with Twitter highlighted as a leading example (Hermida, Twittering the News 297). However, the last decade has seen significant changes in the digital media environment. Twitter, now rebranded as X, has significantly declined in importance as a platform for journalists, politicians, celebrities, and the public. In this article, I revisit the theoretical foundations of ambient journalism. It examines how the concept and its connection to Twitter have evolved since 2010, reflecting the rise and fall of platforms, the increase in algorithmic curation, and the fragmentation of public attention. The decline of Twitter as a central hub for journalistic activity, competition from alternative platforms, the spread of mis- and disinformation, and the role of artificial intelligence in news curation have all contributed to a more complex ambient news environment. This development has shifted ambient journalism from a focus on collective intelligence toward an algorithmically driven stream that privileges affective resonance over narrative continuity, often leading to atomised rather than shared mental models. Looking ahead, the rise and rapid diffusion of generative AI raise questions about whether there is now an epistemological turn toward synthetic ambience. Synthetic ambience describes a phenomenon where human-like AI bots and generative AI summaries reshape how information flows. I analyse these changes, arguing that the move to algorithmic and synthetic ambience requires a critical rethinking of how news is experienced, understood, and verified in a post-Twitter world. The Roots of Ambient Journalism With the emergence and growth of social media platforms in the early 2000s, it became common for the public to share, document, and discuss news, experiences, and opinions. Twitter’s architecture was ideal for news, with its reverse-chronological format, immediacy, follower structure, and the ability to connect diverse messages through hashtags (Burgess and Baym 33). The platform thrived on expectations of sharing and public engagement, as shown in retweets, replies, and direct messages. Reports of major events like plane crashes and earthquakes often first appeared on Twitter, frequently from people involved, with the platform acting as “a nervous system for the planet” (Hermida, Tell Everyone 134). Drawing on the concept of awareness systems from ubiquitous computing, I defined ambient journalism as “an awareness system that offers diverse means to collect, communicate, share and display news and information, serving diverse purposes” (Hermida 301). The concept was developed in the context of Twitter as a media environment characterised by immediacy, serendipity, and the blending of professional and citizen journalism (Goode 1288). The original ambient journalism framework marked a departure from traditional, intentional consumption, like reading a newspaper or watching a TV news bulletin. The main features of ambient journalism were immediacy, unexpected discoveries, and the blending of professional and citizen journalism enabled by Twitter. Its architecture, with a reverse-chronological feed, a 140-character limit, and a social graph-based network structure, supported what Axel Bruns called produsage, where publics act as both users and producers of information. In this ambient environment, retweeting was not just an engagement metric but a curatorial act of journalism. This reflects a broader shift in digital journalism research toward the news ecosystem and landscape, including ambient and networked forms of journalism emerging from social media practices (Steensen and Ahva 1). The ambient news landscape has changed as platforms have developed. Algorithm-driven feeds, misinformation, and alternative sites have made the environment more fragmented and unpredictable. Where Twitter once served as a key, real-time hub during breaking news, from natural disasters to elections, now no single platform fully serves that connecting role. Newsrooms that previously relied on Twitter as an informal wire service now need to monitor multiple platforms, each with its own algorithms and norms. For audiences, losing a shared ambient space means that breaking news no longer appears through a common stream but arrives unevenly, reaching different groups at different times. This results in a more scattered, algorithmically curated mosaic that challenges the idea of ambient journalism and calls for a rethinking and redefining of the term. Networked Publics, Uneven Power The concept of a pervasive, participatory news ecosystem enabled by digital platforms, particularly Twitter, was based on users acting as both producers and consumers of news and information. Driven by the socio-technical structures of social networking sites, users formed networked publics where news was co-created and shared (boyd). Scholars have examined Twitter as a public space for networked publics during events like natural disasters, protests, or political instability, from the Arab Spring to the Occupy movement. The activist movements of the early 2010s exemplified the participatory and collective nature of ambient journalism, with Twitter serving as the link that allowed like-minded individuals to connect, communicate, and organise (Hermida, Tell Everyone 187; Papacharissi 52). Over the past decade, research has questioned the participatory concept of ambient journalism. Scholars have challenged the idea of Twitter as a mirror of public opinion (Mellon and Prosser 6; Mitchell and Hitlin par. 13), even though journalists frequently used tweets as a measure of the vox populi (Beckers and Harder 910). They viewed it as a helpful tool for newsgathering (McGregor and Molyneux 607) or to showcase ordinary people’s opinions (Tworek par. 1). While Twitter promoted an ambient awareness system, ambient journalism overlooked the power disparities between those who are seen and heard on specific issues at certain times (Hermida, Tell Everyone 101). Twitter was never a town square representing all voices. Only a minority of citizens used it, and only a minority of those users were highly active (Leetaru et al. par. 7). Accounts managed by journalists, politicians, celebrities, and bloggers were overrepresented among heavy users and dominated attention, giving them outsized influence on the network. A study on COVID-19 news sharing shows that 14 percent drove most circulation, and these creators accounted for 82 percent of false content in the dataset (Castioni et al. 1). The presence of a small group of active and influential users explains how Twitter became a key arena for highlighting important topics and political signals, even if it did not reflect the views of the broader public (Robertson). This concentration created a steady stream of signals such as retweets, replies, and metrics that spread widely beyond the core participants. Ambient journalism explains why Twitter changed how publics matter. Although the wider effects on the public sphere remain debated, this ambient environment provided a platform for networked publics to influence which stories are told and how, especially for place-based news events (Barnard 14; Bruns and Highfield 332). Taken together, these dynamics demonstrate how ambient journalism affected newsroom judgment in real time by making audience signals more immediate and difficult to ignore, without displacing editorial authority. Shifts in Platform Infrastructure Since 2010, the technological infrastructure underpinning ambient journalism has undergone a radical shift. My original conceptualisation was tethered to the affordances of early Twitter. The contemporary social media landscape, including Twitter, is defined by an algorithmic turn. In March 2016, the platform moved to algorithmic curation based on the presumed interests, preferences, and tastes of users, unless people chose to opt out. This shift transformed the ambient experience from a social utility that helps people keep up with friends and news into a relevance engine optimising passive watch time. For ambient journalism, it represented a significant shift. The ambient news in a feed became less about reflecting the priorities of a user’s connections and more about mirroring their individual habits and cues. While algorithms can help surface engaging content, they can also prioritise sensational or polarising stories over those that serve the public interest (Dujeancourt and Garz 264). The lack of transparency around algorithms exacerbates these issues, as people are often unaware of the biases and incentives shaping their news feeds. They amplify some tweets while reducing the visibility of others, playing a crucial yet hidden role in gatekeeping on Twitter (Huszár et al. 1). The mental model of news and events shifts from being based on the collective intelligence of the network (Malone et al. 2) to the social power of algorithms (Beer 1). Elon Musk’s acquisition of Twitter in 2022 and its rebranding as X in 2023 marked a turning point for ambient journalism, as the billionaire reshaped the socio-technical infrastructures underpinning the platform. He sought to reinvent it as a versatile utility, shifting from its original event-based microblogging to accommodate long-form posts and extended videos. Verification, once a symbol of trust for public figures and journalists, shifted to a subscription model based on payment rather than prominence. Musk has used the rhetoric of free speech to justify dismantling trust-and-safety teams and reinstating previously banned extremist and deceptive accounts. His portrayal as a free speech absolutist is mostly self-promotional branding rather than a consistent principle. In practice, his leadership of X has been characterised by selective enforcement, indicating that his priority is to control narratives rather than to foster open dialogue (See). These structural changes triggered cascading effects on the ambient journalism environment. The dismantling of trust-and-safety infrastructure, along with X’s withdrawal from third-party content-monitoring partnerships, undermined confidence in the platform. A more permissive moderation policy has encouraged a cultural acceptance of extreme and violent rhetoric, further degrading the quality of ambient news streams (de Keulenaar et al.). Many left-leaning academics, journalists, and news organisations reduced their presence on X or moved to alternatives like Bluesky and Mastodon (Ng and Ray 1), hollowing out the very network of professional and civic voices that supported ambient journalism on the platform. The overall result was a more unpredictable, volatile, and untrustworthy news and information environment. Collectively, Musk’s actions tore down Twitter as a place where weak ties formed the connective tissue of public conversation and, more broadly, led to “the waning of ideals in relation to the communicative potentials of the open web more generally” (Tkacz and Gehl 1). Fragmentation, Fatigue, and Fabrication The original framework for ambient journalism proposed that ubiquitous information flows would help citizens develop a mental model of public affairs through peripheral awareness of news content. Twitter was one of many ambient systems that created conditions for incidental exposure to news, with people encountering news passively rather than intentionally seeking it out (Schäfer 242). Ambient journalism predicted that news consumption would become ubiquitous, fragmented, and incidental, but it overestimated how much this peripheral exposure would help build knowledge. Instead, a decade of research shows that for many people, constant connectivity leads to less coherent understanding, increased cognitive fragmentation, and psychological stress (Bawden and Robinson par. 3). Since 2010, the relentless flow of updates has intensified, with a proliferation of platforms, smartphones, and push services, let alone major news events from Brexit to COVID-19 to Trump’s elections. Ambient infrastructures have become too successful, leading to a reaction against the very awareness systems I described. When the flow becomes too much, people have sought to limit exposure, actively avoiding updates and further coverage. While news avoidance is a complex and multilayered phenomenon (Andersen et al. 1367), it is partly driven by feelings of news overload, fatigue, and paralysis (Newman et al. 35). Ambient journalism does not create mental models of news but instead increases the cognitive load on audiences. For many, the sheer volume of news feels overwhelming and harmful to their well-being, with the constant focus of newsrooms on conflict and crisis leading to emotional exhaustion (Toff et al. 3). At the same time, the fragmentation of the social media landscape and the splintering of audiences across different platforms with unique algorithmic logics, content norms, and user demographics mean that ambient journalism can no longer be understood through just one platform. Platforms like TikTok, Instagram, Bluesky, and LinkedIn influence much of the casual news consumption, each offering different features that impact how news is presented, encountered, and interpreted. TikTok’s short videos and Instagram’s visual, story-driven format create experiences that are different from the text-based and conversational style of Twitter. The design logics of TikTok and Instagram have altered how people encounter and make sense of news. TikTok centres an algorithmically driven For You stream that learns from viewing behavior and surfaces clips that fit personal interests. This creates an incidental and episodic flow driven by affect and momentary relevance more than by ongoing narratives or social ties. Similarly, Instagram Stories pull content into a visual, short-lived format, creating a highly visual, ephemeral environment. In a high-choice and fragmented media landscape, the lightweight, always-on nature of ambient journalism can hinder rather than help understanding. These pressures intensify during crises. While mis- and disinformation are far from novel, the networked, digital media environment has amplified the scale, speed, and reach of false information (Waisbord 1866). Ambient news streams have become polluted with a chaotic mix of mis- and disinformation, rumors, hate, and emotional outbursts, making it hard to distinguish between credibility and noise. These trends have accelerated since Musk took control of Twitter, increasing the reach of toxic content. While it was once “widely lambasted as a cesspool of vanity and triviality” (Puschmann et al. 425), it is now condemned as a “sewer” polluted by disinformation, harassment, and hate (Hammond-Errey). The shift from cesspool to sewer marks a notable escalation. Early criticisms focussed on the platform’s obsession with triviality, self-promotion, and banality. Now the criticism emphasises a space where disinformation, coordinated harassment, and hate speech not only coexist with news but actively pollute it. The shift to algorithmic curation favours sensational and polarising content over credible reporting (Dujeancourt and Garz 264), while X has lost the institutional checks that once helped moderate the platform’s most extreme issues. The pay-for-verification model has removed the heuristics that helped users distinguish reliable sources from unreliable ones. The result is a fundamentally changed incentive system where news pollution is financially rewarded, algorithmically amplified, and institutionally unchecked (Hermida et al. par. 1). This weakens trust, spurs inaction and news fatigue, and contributes to polarised views (Hasell and Halversen 1448; Tandoc and Kim 1187). Discussion The core of ambient journalism, that news and information would shift from a discrete product to a pervasive environment, has largely occurred as people encounter these through networked, asynchronous, and always-on communication infrastructures. The background flow of updates from various sources, not solely journalists, has become the primary information environment for many, particularly those under 34 years old (Newman et al. 12). For these audiences, news is less an appointment and more an atmosphere. No single item constitutes the whole story. Instead, people encounter continuously updated slivers of information. The evolution of the media environment and news habits highlights the limitations of the initial, more optimistic framing of ambient journalism. Fragmented attention, mis- or disinformation, and news fatigue have undermined the value of peripheral awareness for audiences. What was once considered a tool for collective intelligence now poses risks of anxiety, fragmentation, and news avoidance. The widespread use of algorithmic curation has reshaped ambient flows, prioritising attention over informing the public. The importance of Twitter has diminished with the rise and growing use of competing platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and AI-powered aggregators. Musk’s purchase and the era of X have increased uncertainty about how news is surfaced and consumed, creating a more unpredictable environment where visibility can surpass verification. As many journalists and news organisations reduce their use of X (Ng and Ray 1), its role as a place for ambient awareness, especially during breaking news, becomes more contested and less central than it was in the 2010s. It is even questionable whether X remains a platform for ambient journalism. Algorithmic curation on X now actively de-prioritises quality journalism in favour of content that maximises engagement, while the platform’s financial incentive structure rewards the most attention-grabbing posts regardless of their veracity or public value (Dujeancourt and Garz 264). The dismantling of trust-and-safety infrastructure, the shift to pay-for-verification, and the reinstatement of previously banned accounts have collectively undermined the good-faith assumptions on which ambient journalism relied. In these conditions, what persists on X may be better understood as ambient information noise rather than ambient journalism. In a sense, the epistemological foundations that justified the term journalism have been so thoroughly compromised that the label risks lending unwarranted legitimacy to a deeply polluted environment. X still generates ambient awareness, but whether that awareness constitutes a form of journalism, even in the loose, peripheral sense originally intended, is highly questionable. Ambient journalism has, then, outgrown Twitter, prompting a re-evaluation of the concept to address the complex, algorithmically mediated, and uneven ways in which news is encountered and understood, incorporating affective resonance (Mühlhoff 1001). To account for the socio-technical and cultural changes since 2010, ambient journalism can now be defined as incidentally consumed, algorithmically guided streams of episodic news and information fragments that privilege affective resonance over narrative continuity, fostering atomised rather than collective mental models of public events. This definition recognises an epistemological shift in the infrastructures of news and information. It captures the shift from receiving news and information in fixed editions from authoritative sources to a persistent, pervasive flow of snippets from numerous voices. Outlook Recent and ongoing socio-technical advances in artificial intelligence could ultimately challenge the very concept of ambient news. Here, I introduce the term synthetic ambience, which describes a phenomenon where the ambient news environment is increasingly shaped by AI-generated content that mimics the look and rhythms of human journalism, rather than by human journalists or networked publics. AI-generated signals imitate, compress, and ultimately replace the organic flows that ambient journalism once relied on. Synthetic ambience suggests an epistemological turn, signaling a move from social ambience, rooted in networked publics, to algorithmic ambience, driven by platform curation. What distinguishes synthetic ambience from its earlier concept is the dual processes of fabrication and compression. AI-created bots and automated accounts flood information streams with fake social proof and emotional signals that shape perception. AI-produced summaries and chatbot responses shorten the diverse, multi-source signals that formed ambient journalism into a single, pre-processed synthesis, bypassing traditional human sense-making. Fabrication pollutes the system’s inputs, the signals, while compression interferes with the outputs, the cognitive processes citizens use to piece together meaning from fragments. Together, they reshape an information landscape that maintains the constant, all-pervasive nature of ambient journalism while removing its epistemic core. As a concept, synthetic ambience lies at the crossroads of journalism studies, platform studies, AI ethics, and the epistemology of information. It addresses growing concerns about how generative AI not only transforms content creation but also the very infrastructure of public knowledge, effectively creating ambient layers through which publics develop impressions of the world. The fabrication mechanism is already here. While bots have always been present on Twitter (Carlon et al.), advances in generative AI suggest that platforms like X can expect to be flooded by AI bots that are nearly indistinguishable from real accounts (Yang and Menczer 20). Where earlier forms of astroturfing and coordinated inauthentic behaviour were labour-intensive and easier to detect, generative AI now enables the mass creation of synthetic voices that imitate the tone, cadence, and emotional range of genuine users in ways that are procedurally indistinguishable from organic activity. AI bots on X and other platforms turn ambient journalism into synthetic ambience by polluting streams with a large volume of posts. Emotional triggers and synthetic social proof can create the illusion of consensus or outrage on a much larger scale than previous attempts to influence public opinion. Synthetic ambience risks overshadowing verified reporting through intentional news pollution, diverting attention and mimicking emotion at scale. The result is a reduction in viewpoint diversity and erosion of trust, benefiting those with the resources to use AI extensively for these purposes. The compression mechanism operates through a different but complementary logic. Since Google introduced AI-generated summaries in 2025, such rundowns have become one of the most common ways to interact with generative AI (Simon 57). The immediacy, convenience, and perceived veracity of the summaries are most appealing to audiences (36). Algorithmic compression, through AI-generated summaries or responses from AI chatbots like ChatGPT and Perplexity, collapses the diverse, multi-source signals that make up ambient journalism into a single, synthesised answer, shaped by the AI model’s weighting. Audiences receive a ready-made interpretation influenced by a model, rather than encountering multiple voices and gradually forming their own understanding. Algorithmic compression both models and usurps human editorial authority. AI summaries and chatbot answers offer a pre-assembled model, effectively ending the ambient journalism cycle by taking over the cognitive labour of synthesis and producing a processed sense of events. The risk is not just homogenisation but also epistemic displacement, where publics never experience the diversity of reporting, sourcing, and perspectives that once defined the ambient news environment. This makes it harder to spot gaps, contradictions, or contested claims within the information landscape. Future research could explore whether there is now an epistemological shift toward synthetic ambience. Conceptually, future work should examine whether synthetic ambience operates differently across platforms and cultural contexts, and whether its dual mechanisms of fabrication and compression produce distinct or compounding effects on public knowledge formation. As ambient journalism continues to outgrow the platform that nurtured it, there remains a need to understand how people learn and interpret news within systems shaped by networks, algorithms, and generative AI. The rise of AI-powered synthetic ambience points to an information environment that is persistent, pervasive, and increasingly processed by machine models rather than human minds. If the ambient journalism of the 2010s described how citizens understood the world through the peripheral hum of networked voices, synthetic ambience suggests an emerging situation where that hum is increasingly generated, curated, and condensed by machines, raising questions about what it means to be informed in an age of artificial intelligence. title: Cybersecurity in Action
authors: Somayye Nasiri; Shahram Shahabi; Aynaz Shafiesabet; Maral Talebbeidokhti; Elmira Alvandi Behineh
Tags: organizational factors; cybersecurity framework; data analysis; public sector
abstract: This research aimed to develop and assess a framework for exploring how factors at the organizational, social, and individual levels influence cybersecurity behavior (CB), with a particular focus on the intermediary role played by awareness of cybersecurity. Participants in this investigation included 381 employees from various public sector entities, with data being gathered via a questionnaire. The analysis was conducted through SEM, specifically employing the PLS. Outputs from this study indicate that cybersecurity awareness (CA), security self-efficacy (SSE), the perceived threat severity, and the perceived vulnerability all positively and significantly impact CBs. Additionally, the research found that initiatives focused on security training and awareness, information security culture, social media influence, family and friends influence, and information systems knowledge all significantly contribute to enhancing CA. Similarly, these elements, alongside direct experience in information security, were shown to affec SSE, with the exception of the influence exerted by family and friends, which did not show a significant impact on it. Influences from social media, family and friends, and direct experiences with information security were also found to significantly affect the perceived severity of threats and perceived of vulnerability. Thus, this study concludes that the factors analyzed are strong indicators of behavior related to cybersecurity.
Keywords: cybersecurity behavior; cybersecurity awareness; security self-efficacy; social media influence
affiliations: Islamic Azad University of Zanjan; Islamic Azad University North Tehran Branch; University of Houston; University of New Haven; University of the Cumberlands

title: Launch a Smart AI Grocery Delivery App for Instant Deliveries
authors: Swiza joy
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Forums & Communities; Corporate Records
Tags: grocery app; AI technology; delivery logistics; market analysis; user engagement
abstract: In recent years, the grocery delivery industry has experienced a significant transformation, driven by advancements in technology and changing consumer behaviors. As people increasingly seek convenience and instant access to essentials, the demand for smart grocery delivery apps has surged. With the rise of artificial intelligence, these applications are not only streamlining the shopping experience but also optimizing logistics for faster deliveries. This article explores the intricacies of launching a smart AI grocery delivery app, detailing market demand, essential features, technological foundations, user experience design, logistics management, marketing strategies, data security, and future trends shaping the industry. Understanding the Market Demand for Grocery Delivery Apps Current Trends in Grocery Shopping Grocery shopping is undergoing a digital revolution, and it’s about as trendy as a pair of high-waisted jeans. Consumers are moving toward online shopping primarily due to convenience, especially in the era of busy lifestyles and the ever-looming threat of grocery store lines. With an increasing number of people opting for delivery services, the demand for grocery delivery apps is skyrocketing faster than you can say, "Let's skip the store.” Consumer Preferences and Pain Points Today's consumers crave speed and simplicity, much like their coffee orders. They want apps that are easy to navigate, provide accurate product availability, and offer quick deliveries. Pain points such as late deliveries, missing items, and poor user experiences can leave shoppers feeling more frustrated than they do when trying to assemble IKEA furniture. Identifying these preferences is your golden ticket to a winning grocery app. Comparative Analysis of Existing Services In a world filled with grocery delivery apps, it can feel like speed dating—pick the right one, or you'll be left in a cart full of regrets. By analyzing existing services, you can spot the trends, success stories, and the calamities to avoid. From big players like Instacart to niche services, each has its strengths and weaknesses. A little competitive research can help you carve out your unique selling proposition and become the app everyone raves about (and not just to vent frustrations). Key Features of a Smart AI Grocery Delivery App Real-Time Inventory Management Imagine knowing exactly what’s in stock without stepping foot in a store. Real-time inventory management is not just a feature; it's like having a personal grocery assistant who never takes a coffee break. This capability helps ensure customers avoid the disappointment of ordering a product that’s as elusive as a unicorn. Personalized Shopping Experience Picture this: your app suggests recipes based on what users purchased last week or even their dietary preferences. A personalized shopping experience, powered by AI, can turn a mundane task into a culinary adventure. Think of it as the app that knows what you like better than your best friend (sorry, Susan)! Smart Pricing and Promotions Who doesn’t love a good deal? Smart pricing algorithms can analyze shopping patterns and offer personalized promotions that make customers feel like they’re snagging a treasure. With dynamic pricing, your app can adjust to demand fluctuations as nimbly as a cat avoiding a dog. Seamless Payment Options In the age of near-instant gratification, a seamless payment process is crucial. Integrating various payment methods, including digital wallets, credit cards, and maybe even a good old-fashioned cash option (for the nostalgic), can make checking out as smooth as butter. No one wants to fight with payment options while imagining their ice cream melting. Technology Stack: Building the App with AI Capabilities Choosing the Right Programming Language Selecting the right programming language is like choosing the perfect pair of shoes for a big night out: it can make all the difference. Java, Python, or even JavaScript could be your allies in building a robust grocery delivery app. Each has its pros and cons, so choose wisely, young grasshopper. AI and Machine Learning Algorithms To make your app smart, you'll need AI and machine learning algorithms that can analyze user behavior and improve over time. Think of it as giving your app a brain, allowing it to learn and adapt—like a toddler, but much less likely to throw tantrums. Cloud Infrastructure and Scalability Cloud services are like the backbone of your app, providing the necessary muscle for growth and scalability. Using a cloud-based infrastructure means you can handle a sudden influx of users without breaking a sweat (or the app). Whether you choose AWS or Google Cloud, make sure you're set for the long haul. Integrating Third-Party Services From payment gateways to logistics providers, integrating third-party services will make your app's life easier. It’s like assembling an all-star team: each has its role, and together, they make your app shine brighter than a diamond in a pile of potatoes. User Experience Design for Seamless Navigation Intuitive Interface Design Principles An intuitive interface means users can find what they need without needing a treasure map. Keep it simple and user-friendly because if a user has to think too hard, they might just decide to make a sandwich instead of browsing your app. Mobile vs. Web Experience Designing for mobile and web is like choosing between tacos and a burrito—both delicious but offer different experiences. The mobile interface should be touch-friendly and quick, while the web version could provide a bit more information. Ensure that both are cohesive so users can switch between them as easily as they switch between their favorite streaming platforms. Accessibility Considerations The best user experience is one that everyone can enjoy, regardless of their abilities. Implementing accessibility features is not just good practice; it’s also kind. From text-to-speech options to easy navigation, making your app accessible ensures that it resonates with a broader audience—and that’s a win for everyone! Logistics supply chain management for instant deliveries. Optimizing Delivery Routes with AI In the fast-paced world of grocery delivery, every second counts. Enter AI, the digital wizard that can optimize delivery routes faster than you can say "Where's my avocado toast?" By analyzing real-time traffic data, weather patterns, and even local events, AI can map out the quickest routes, minimizing delays and ensuring your customers get their organic kale just in time for dinner. Partnerships with Local Stores and Suppliers Building strong partnerships with local stores and suppliers is like assembling the Avengers of grocery delivery. These alliances ensure your app has a wide selection of fresh products while keeping delivery times as short as a TikTok video. Collaborating with local businesses also boosts community support, making your app the neighborhood hero while helping local grocers thrive in a digital world. Handling Delivery Logistics and Timing Timing is everything, especially when you're delivering groceries. Efficient logistics management ensures that orders are processed, packed, and shipped with the precision of a Swiss watch. Implementing a real-time tracking system lets customers know their groceries are on the way, alleviating the anxiety of wondering if the ice cream will arrive before dinner (spoiler: it should!). Marketing Strategies to Attract Users Leveraging Social Media and Influencer Marketing Social media is the megaphone of the modern age, and your grocery app should be shouting from the rooftops! Collaborating with influencers who have a passion for food can make your app the talk of the town. Eye-catching posts, delicious photos, and engaging stories will attract potential users like moths to a flame—just make sure the flame is a cozy, pizza-scented one! Referral Programs and Incentives Everyone loves free stuff, and referral programs are the golden ticket. Encourage your current users to share the love by offering discounts or freebies for every friend they snag. It’s a win-win: they get to enjoy their local groceries, and you can grow your user base faster than a cat video goes viral. Local Advertising and Community Engagement Nothing beats good old-fashioned community engagement. Partner with local events, sponsor a farmers’ market, or organize grocery-themed challenges. Local advertising—whether it’s flyers, community boards, or good ol’ word of mouth—can make your app a household name in no time. After all, everyone loves a grocery delivery service that feels like a friendly neighbor! Ensuring Data Security and Privacy in AI Applications Understanding Data Compliance Regulations Navigating data compliance regulations is like trying to follow a GPS that keeps recalculating. Familiarizing yourself with laws like GDPR and CCPA ensures you’re not only following the rules but also building trust with your users. Transparency about how you handle their data can help users feel more secure and willing to share their grocery preferences (and maybe their favorite pizza topping). Implementing Secure Payment Systems With great convenience comes great responsibility. Implementing secure payment systems is crucial to ensuring your users’ financial information stays in Fort Knox and out of prying eyes. Think encryption, secure payment gateways, and regular audits—because nobody wants to wake up to a morning of identity theft alongside their avocado toast. Protecting User Information and Privacy User privacy is the crown jewel of any app, and protecting it should be a top priority. Implement strategies that go beyond the basics: anonymizing data, allowing users to control their information, and keeping communication channels clear and open. The more secure users feel, the more likely
Keywords: AI; grocery delivery; logistics; user experience; data security

title: Designing Extended Intelligence: A Taxonomy of Psychobiological Effects of XR–AI Systems for Human Capability Augmentation
authors: Jolanda Tromp; Ilias El Makrini; Mario Trógolo; Miguel A. Muñoz; Maria B. Sánchez-Barrerra; Jose Pech Pacheco; Cándida Castro
Tags: XR; AI; Human-Centered Design; Cyber-Physical Systems; TAXI-XI-CAP Framework
abstract: Extended Reality (XR) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) are increasingly converging within cyber–physical infrastructures, including digital twins, the Spatial Web, and smart-city systems. These environments require new frameworks for understanding how human performance emerges through sustained interaction with immersive interfaces and adaptive computational agents. This paper introduces the TAXI–XI-CAP framework, a two-layer model that links psychobiological mechanisms of XR–AI interaction to higher-level, experimentally testable capability constructs. The TAXI layer defines 42 mechanisms spanning perception, cognition, physiology, sensorimotor control, and social coordination, while XI-CAP organizes these into capability patterns such as remote dexterity, distributed cognition, and adaptive workload regulation. Derived through a theory-guided synthesis across XR, neuroscience, and human–automation interaction, the framework models performance as emerging from interacting mechanisms under real-world constraints. A validation-oriented research agenda is proposed, emphasizing mechanism-level measurement, capability-level evaluation, and longitudinal testing. The TAXI–XI-CAP framework provides a structured basis for hypothesis generation, comparative analysis, and empirical validation of XR–AI systems, supporting the development of reliable, scalable, and human-centered Extended Intelligence infrastructures.
Keywords: Extended Reality; Artificial Intelligence; Human Capability Augmentation; Psychobiological Effects; Capability Evaluation
affiliations: Duy Tan University; Vrije Universiteit Brussel; Universidad Siglo 21; Universidad de Granada; Hospital Universitario Araba; Centro de Tecnologías Aeronauticas (Spain)

title: AI-Powered Cyber Defense: Next-Gen Threat Detection Using Machine & Deep Learning
venue: International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology & Science
Tags: Cyber Defense; Zero-Day Exploits; Advanced Persistent Threats; Behavioral Analysis; Precision/Recall Metrics
abstract: Cyber threats are evolving rapidly, outpacing traditional security systems against zero-day exploits and APTs.This research proposes an AI-driven framework using machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) to automate real-time threat detection, prediction, and prevention.It addresses critical gaps like high false positives and slow response times by developing novel ML/DL algorithms for anomaly detection through behavioral analysis.A multi-layered defense architecture integrates supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, trained on datasets (e.g., CIC-IDS, NSL-KDD) to identify malware, phishing, and intrusions.Techniques include Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for attack patterns, auto encoders for traffic anomalies, and Explainable AI (XAI) for transparency.The system employs adversarial training to resist evasion and federated learning for privacy-preserving authentication.Evaluations use precision/recall metrics, latency benchmarks, and adversarial stress tests, targeting a 50% reduction in false positives and submillisecond response times.Scalability is tested across cloud/edge environments, with lightweight models for IoT.Threat intelligence (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK) enables continuous retraining against APTs.The framework complies with GDPR/NIST standards and outperforms signature-based tools in simulations.Applications span finance, healthcare, and critical infrastructure.By bridging human expertise and autonomous AI, this research aims to redefine cyber defense paradigms.Findings will be shared via peer-reviewed publications and opensource prototypes.
Keywords: AI-driven framework; Machine Learning; Deep Learning; Threat Detection; Anomaly Detection; Graph Neural Networks; Explainable AI; Federated Learning

title: Disinformation, Psychosocial Vulnerability, and Media Trust in the Digital Era: Implications for Health Behaviour and Societal Resilience
authors: João Miguel Alves Ferreira; Vaitsa Giannouli; Sergii Tukaiev
Tags: Public Health; Crisis Communication; Media Literacy; Digital Platforms
abstract: Disinformation, amplified by digital platforms and algorithmic distribution systems, represents a growing challenge for media trust, public health communication, and societal stability. This narrative literature review examines disinformation through an integrative psychosocial perspective, focusing on how patterns of exposure interact with individual vulnerability factors—including education, political beliefs, social identity, personality traits, and emotional responses to uncertainty—to influence the processing and acceptance of misleading information. The review synthesises interdisciplinary evidence on how algorithmic amplification and emotionally salient content increase susceptibility to disinformation and shape risk perception, health-related decision-making, and preventive behaviours. Findings indicate that repeated exposure to false or misleading information reinforces perceived credibility through familiarity effects, contributes to declining trust in institutional sources, and intensifies social and political polarisation. Disinformation is therefore conceptualised not only as an informational problem but also as a psychosocial process affecting emotional regulation, cognitive evaluation, and collective responses to crises, particularly in public health contexts. The analysis further highlights a recursive feedback loop in which reduced media trust increases vulnerability to subsequent disinformation, with broader implications for democratic participation and social cohesion. Mitigation strategies discussed include media literacy initiatives, critical thinking education, platform governance, regulatory approaches, and interventions targeting psychosocial drivers of susceptibility.
Keywords: Disinformation; Media Trust; Health Behavior; Societal Resilience; Psychosocial Vulnerability
affiliations: University of Coimbra; Democritus University of Thrace; Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv; Università della Svizzera italiana

title: The Datafied Child: A Systematic Literature Review and Research Agenda on Sharenting, Privacy, and Social Media
authors: Kyle Bittle; Cherie Noteboom
venue: ACM SIGMIS Database: the DATABASE for Advances in Information Systems
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; Government Data
Tags: Children's Privacy; Ethics; Information Systems; Platform Accountability; Interdisciplinary Research
abstract: Sharenting, the parental practice of sharing children's personal information online, raises critical privacy, ethical, and regulatory concerns. This systematic literature review and research agenda examines how sharenting is studied, analyzing theoretical frameworks through an information systems (IS) lens. Findings identify five key themes—privacy management, identity presentation, consumer vulnerability, social influence, and parenting ethics—shaped by broader technological, social, and regulatory forces. To structure these insights, this study introduces a three-domain framework to categorize how digital infrastructures, cultural norms, and policy gaps influence sharenting behaviors. The review highlights limited IS engagement in sharenting research and underscores the need for greater focus on platform accountability, digital consent mechanisms, and parental decision-making. By mapping key gaps, this study advances an interdisciplinary research agenda while presenting a critical opportunity to apply IS theories and frameworks to inform policy, platform design, and digital literacy efforts that safeguard children's privacy and autonomy in online spaces.
Keywords: Sharenting; Privacy; Social Media; Digital Consent; Parental Decision-Making

title: A Hybrid Machine Learning–Deep Learning Framework for Explainable and Scalable Digital Forensic Analysis
authors: Soni Rameshrao Ragho; Narendra Chaudhari
venue: International Journal of Technology & Emerging Research
Tags: Digital Forensics; Cybersecurity; AI in Forensics; Real-time Analysis; Complex Data Handling
abstract: The intensive development of new digital technologies, cloud computing, and networked systems made the amount and complexity of the digital evidence in cases of cybercrime investigation significantly greater. Manual and rule-based digital forensic techniques cannot manage large-scale heterogeneous and real-time data environments. Such systems are not always scalable, interpretable, and robust, which restricts their applicability in the current cyber threats. To address these issues, this paper suggests a Hybrid AI-Based Forensic Intelligence Framework that could be used to analyze digital evidence in scales and provide an explanation and real-time analysis. The suggested framework will combine some of the latest methods of artificial intelligence, such as machine learning, deep learning, and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), to automate and improve the process of forensics. It helps in preprocessing data, feature extractions, anomaly detection, correlation of evidence and transparent decision making. The system can effectively handle a wide range of sources of data including system logs, network traffic, and multimedia artifacts using scalable hybrid models. Also, explainability properties provide legal reliability and transparency of forensic results. The experimental findings indicate that there are better accuracy, scalability, and reliability as opposed to traditional tools and single-model solutions. On the whole, the framework offers a powerful and intelligent approach to digital forensics in the modern context related to the investigation and making decisions more efficient in a complex cybercrime situation.
Keywords: Hybrid AI Framework; Digital Forensics; Machine Learning; Deep Learning; Explainable AI; Scalability; Cybercrime; Data Analysis

title: Effectiveness of the Daniel Model in Enhancing Primary Students’ Learning of Cybersecurity Concepts
authors: Ali A. Al-Barakat; Rommel M. AlAli; Omayya M. Al-Hassan; Eid M. Kanaan; Yusra Z. Aboud; Ali K. Abdullatif
Tags: Educational Research; Cybersecurity; Student Learning; Teaching Methods
abstract: This study investigated the effectiveness of Daniel's Model in teaching cybersecurity concepts to ninth-grade students in Amman, Jordan. A quasi-experimental design with two groups was employed; the experimental group received instruction based on Daniel's model, while the control group followed traditional teaching methods. The sample consisted of 120 students, was selected from ten international schools via convenience sampling, and was randomly assigned to the groups. Data were collected using a 33-item knowledge test covering four key domains of cybersecurity. Analysis in SPSS included an independent samples t-test and a paired samples t-test; in addition, effect size (η²) was calculated to understand the magnitude of the model impact. A higher score of digital threat knowledge and skills in terms of prevention, ethical behaviors, and data protection related to the model taught was noticeable among the experimental group, since the effect size (η²) varied from large (0.34) to very large (0.62). Also, in the delayed test, the experimental group showed no decline in performance, demonstrating that the model was effective in teaching knowledge and digital practices. Considering the results, it is recommended that Daniel's Model, combined with interactive techniques and scenario building, be integrated in teaching digital ethics and use of technology, in addition to training teachers on systematic implementation of desired, sustained outcomes of the model.
Keywords: Daniel's Model; Cybersecurity Education; Digital Ethics; Interactive Techniques
affiliations: University of Sharjah; King Faisal University; Qatar University

title: Spatiotemporal deep learning for real-time video-based deepfake detection using 3DCNN, 3DResNet, TCN, and VAE
authors: Prateek Agrawal; Dharmendra Pathak; Vishu Madaan; Pawan Kumar Verma; Wou Onn Choo
Tags: AI; video analysis; security; privacy; machine learning
abstract: Advanced AI techniques enable the creation of deepfake videos which endanger digital security and produce misinformation while threatening individual privacy rights. The detection and classification of manipulated content represent an immediate priority for deep learning and computer vision research. Generative adversarial networks (GANs) and deep learning models have experienced rapid development which resulted in deepfakes becoming more realistic than ever before thus making them hard to distinguish from genuine media for both human observers and standard detection systems. The situation becomes worse due to easy access to deepfake generation tools and the advancing complexity of forgeries and the dangerous applications in political manipulation and financial deception and identity theft. Deepfake detection needs powerful AI-based solutions which can handle the evolving deepfake synthesis methods while providing accurate results that generalize to various dataset types. This research paper investigates how four deep learning models including 3D Convolutional Neural Network (3DCNN), 3D Residual Network (3DResNet), Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN), and Variational Autoencoder (VAE) perform in detecting and classifying deepfake videos. The models received training and evaluation using FaceForensics++ (FF++) and Deepfake Detection Challenge (DFDC) and Celeb-DF (CDF) datasets which contained more than 3500 real and fake video samples. The experiments took place on an NVIDIA DGX A100 workstation to achieve efficient model training. This work does not present a new architecture of detection but rather poses itself as a systematic benchmarking and generalization study. The experimental findings indicate that 3DCNN can reach the highest test accuracy of 64.68% which is higher than the results of 3DResNet, TCN, and VAE under the cross-dataset conditions. It analyses indicate that there is significant degradation in generalization and different failure behavior with models when presented with heterogeneous distributions of data. The results offer useful information about the shortcomings of generally embraced spatiotemporal models and guide the creation of more dependable deepfake detection systems that can be implemented in real life.
Keywords: deepfake detection; deep learning; spatiotemporal models; video manipulation; GANs
affiliations: INTI International University; Chhattisgarh Swami Vivekanand Technical University; SRM University; Indian Institute of Technology Bhilai; Symbiosis International University

title: A Comprehensive Survey on Anomaly Detection Techniques for Suspicious File Migration in Cloud Computing Environments
authors: Anurag Tyagi; Atul Saini; Ayush Garg; Ayush Gupta; Priyanka
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: AWS; Data Security; Insider Threat; Deep Learning; Automation
abstract: Cloud storage platforms, particularly Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3), are widely adopted by organizations globally due to their inherent flexibility, massive scalability, and cost-effectiveness. However, the paradigm shift toward distributed cloud infrastructure has exponentially increased the risk of unauthorized or abnormal file transfers. Typical file migrations within these distributed environments have escalated security challenges, where illegal file transfers serve as primary indicators of insider abuse, credential compromise, or systematic data exfiltration. Manually identifying such suspicious migrations is virtually impossible due to the vast volume of daily activity logs. This comprehensive survey investigates state-of-the-art anomaly detection techniques designed to identify suspicious file migration patterns. We critically evaluate traditional rule-based monitoring, deep learning methods, and blockchain-based auditing models. Special emphasis is placed on lightweight, unsupervised machine learning architectures—specifically the Isolation Forest algorithm—which autonomously identify deviations in user activity, file access patterns, and data movement rates by ingesting AWS CloudTrail logs. Furthermore, we explore the end-to-end integration of these models with real-time visualization dashboards built using Flask, enabling automated feature extraction, model serialization via joblib, and rapid threat mitigation by Security Operations Centers (SOC). By analyzing the accuracy, scalability, and operational efficiency of existing frameworks, this survey identifies persistent systemic flaws, such as high false-positive rates and the lack of real-time multi-cloud monitoring. Ultimately, this study proposes dynamic, automated solutions that seamlessly combine cloud computing telemetry, machine learning, and cybersecurity principles to protect mission-critical digital assets across the finance, healthcare, and educational sectors.
Keywords: Anomaly Detection; Cloud Computing; File Migration; Machine Learning; Security Operations

title: From molecule to bedside and back: the integration of computational and experimental physiology in the digital age
authors: Viviane Timmermann; Axel Loewe
Tags: Cardiology; Computational Models; Biomedical Research; Digital Health; Cardiac Arrhythmias
abstract: Historically, physiology has evolved from observational descriptions to the rigorous dissection of mechanisms. Today, we witness another transformation where experimental and computational methodologies are no longer separate disciplines, but rather interconnected strategies that are essential for navigating the nonlinear complexity of the heart. This Special Issue represents a maturation of the ‘Physiome Project’ vision (Loewe et al.; 2025): a quantitative, multi-scale description of physiological dynamics, now spanning from the metabolic state of a crossbridge to the haemodynamic flow in a fibrillating atrium. The contributions in this collection largely stem from the Cardiac Physiome Workshop 2024 in Freiburg, Germany. There, the community gathered to discuss the bidirectional crosstalk between theoretical foundations and specific clinical challenges, such as atrial fibrillation and drug safety. Indeed, we see a broadening of the use of models, which become predictive engines capable of exploring the design of novel therapies, the assessment of clinical risks in real time, and the scaling of physiology from molecule to organ. Thus, successful physiological insights now emerge from the iterative feedback between laboratory data and mathematical reconstruction. The interdisciplinary methodological frameworks highlighting this integration have been reviewed by Yang & Sobie (2025), who focus on defining the essential ‘ingredients’ for the effective integration of computational and experimental approaches. A central, recurring theme in this issue is the emergence of the digital twin. While this term is often used to describe patient-specific modelling in a clinical setting, several contributions in this collection emphasise that the concept also encompasses fundamental biophysical models. These mechanistic twins, though perhaps not tailored to a single individual, provide the necessary framework to interpret experimental variability and define the evolutionary robustness of cardiac function. This integration is crucially bidirectional: while computational models predict outcomes, high-resolution experimental and clinical mapping remains the basis that informs and constrains the next generation of in silico studies. In a seminal contribution, Noble (2026a) traces the theoretical foundations of our current understanding of cardiac rhythm and the models we use to simulate it. By reflecting on the evolution of pacemaker models, Noble demonstrates how mathematical frameworks did not just follow experimental discovery but often pointed toward the necessary experimental questions. This work is further contextualised by Maltsev et al. (2026), whose perspective highlights the evolutionary robustness of these physiological systems, which is a concept essential for understanding why certain rhythms persist or fail under pathological stress. The ongoing dialogue in this field is further reinforced by the commentary of Noble (2026b), which reminds us that, as our models become more complex, we must remain grounded in the fundamental biophysical principles that govern cardiac automaticity. Atrial fibrillation (AF) remains the most common sustained arrhythmia and its structural basis and maintenance mechanisms remain subjects of intense investigation. A major challenge in treating AF is the scale mismatch between the microscopic environment, where triggers arise, and the macroscopic mapping performed in clinical electrophysiology laboratories. Several works in this Special Issue explore how new high-resolution imaging and computational reconstruction technologies are bridging this divide, moving the field towards a more nuanced understanding of how structure influences function. Kulathilaka et al. (2026) exemplify the importance of atrial microstructure by implementing digital models derived from micron-resolution imaging of the human heart. They demonstrate that re-entrant drivers are not randomly distributed but are anchored to specific microstructural features. In an accompanying Perspective, Potse (2026) discusses how these findings suggest that AF drivers ‘like to live in narrow spots’, effectively linking the multiple wavelet and rotor hypotheses by showing how chaotic activity can be organised by anatomical constraints. This microstructural analysis is further explored in a Journal Club article by Cutler (2026), who examines the transition from microstructure to arrhythmia and the specific structural drivers that sustain re-entrant activity. Complementing these ex vivo insights, Horrach et al. (2026) provide a translational review of mapping techniques for evaluating fibrosis in patients. They discuss how various imaging modalities, beyond standard electroanatomic mapping, can characterise the pathological substrate in vivo, thereby bridging the gap between high-resolution histology and clinical catheter ablation. Beyond fixed structural obstacles, the dynamic, functional remodelling of ion channels provides a secondary, evolving substrate for arrhythmia. Meier et al. (2026) applied computational modelling to explore the rate-dependent trafficking of small-conductance calcium-activated potassium (SK) channels. The collection also points out that AF is not solely an electrical phenomenon but one with profound haemodynamic consequences. Bäck et al. (2026) demonstrate how virtual interventions, such as left atrial appendage occlusion, can predict improvements in blood flow and reduce stasis risk, offering a tool for pre-procedural planning. This work is discussed by Hassan et al. (2026). Perhaps the most direct clinical application of these ‘digital heart’ tools is seen in the prospective in silico trial by Dasí et al. (2026). By simulating drug effects across a population of models representing inter-individual variability, they identified combined SK and two-pore-domain potassium channel blockade as a potentially effective strategy for AF cardioversion. This approach illustrates how computational physiology can ‘de-risk’ pharmaceutical development by identifying synergistic targets and predicting outcomes before a single patient is enrolled in a trial. This approach further supports the translational promise of targeting the SK channel, as comprehensively reviewed by Zheng et al. (2026). In the ventricles, the focus shifts to the subtle interplay between activation sequences, repolarisation heterogeneity, and arrhythmia vulnerability. Refining the interpretation of non-invasive data, Smits et al. (2026) present evidence contrary to the assumption that late-activated regions always display shortened action potential durations to maintain T-wave concordance. To correctly interpret electrocardiographic imaging maps in patients with heart failure, they propose a computational strategy challenging longstanding clinical assumptions. The connection between physiological gradients and arrhythmia risk is further explored by Sobota et al. (2026), who show that the apicobasal repolarisation gradient diminishes or inverts with age. This loss of the natural gradient may paradoxically increase vulnerability to re-entrant arrhythmias, providing a potential mechanistic link between the ageing process and the broader societal challenge of managing arrhythmic risk in an increasingly ageing population. In safety pharmacology, models are proving increasingly valuable for predicting cardiotoxicity and minimising the risks associated with specific therapies. Villar-Valero et al. (2026) present a parametric study using three-dimensional (3D) magnetic resonance imaging-based models to predict the effects of doxorubicin. By computationally modelling the specific patterns of diffuse interstitial fibrosis induced by the drug, they identify this structural remodelling as a key biomarker that promotes conduction block. As noted by Durço et al. (2026) in the accompanying Journal Club, this illustrates how the passive collagen network dictates the propagation of electrical waves. Chabiniok & Zaha (2026) expand upon this theme by reviewing the application of cardiac digital twins in assessing chemotherapy-induced substrates. Mora et al. (2026) apply electromechanical simulations to assess the effectiveness of omecamtiv mecarbil in treating heart failure. As discussed by Malicki et al. (2025), this approach offers a well-controlled in silico environment to dissect drug effects in the absence of other systemic influences. The link between metabolism and mechanics is a critical determinant of dysfunction in conditions such as diabetic cardiomyopathy. Musgrave et al. (2026) address this phenomenon with metabolite-sensitive crossbridge models to investigate how the metabolic environment in diabetes directly alters myofilament energetics and stiffness. Such models are important in providing a rigorous thermodynamic framework to explain how changes in metabolite concentrations, often seen in diabetes, impact muscle mechanoenergetics at the molecular level. Complementing this subcellular perspective, Bertrand et al. (2026) review cardiac remodelling in type 2 diabetes across multiple scales, discussing how computational multiscale modelling can link these subcellular defects with tissue-level fibrosis and autonomic neuropathy, thereby improving our understanding of disease progression. At the cellular level, Folkmanaite et al. (2026) demonstrate the value of computational approaches by utilising electromechanical modelling of human-induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes to predict drug-induced contractility effects. In structural heart disease, Dvoulety & Sitina (2026) propose a haemodynamic–energetic mechanism for sudden cardiac death in aortic stenosis. Their model describes a state of ‘cardiac stalling’, whereby the energy demand of the hypertrophied ventricle exceeds the supply during stress, leading to mechanical failure. Townsend (2026) emphasises that this energy-balance framework explains syncope that purely hydraulic models may miss, pointing out the ability of models to assess catastrophic events in silico that cannot be ethically induced in human subjects. Moreover, models are increasingly capable of predicting long-term structural adaptation. Sharifi et al. (2026) incorporated multiscale finite element modelling to elucidate the role of the baroreflex feedback loop in ventricular growth, connecting neural control to tissue mechanics. Moscoloni et al. (2026) further recognise the significance of considering biological variables frequently disregarded in generic models: sex alone explains at least 25% of ventricular morphological variability. These fundamental shape disparities between male and female hearts should be incorporated into future patient-specific or sex-specific simulations. Finally, the developmental origins of these conditions are explored by Bueno-Palomeque et al. (2026), who use a synthesis of clinical and computational data to characterise preadolescents born with intrauterine growth restriction. Moving from pharmacological triggers to structural substrates, this work demonstrates how multi-scale modelling can isolate the specific contributions of ionic remodelling to the developing heart, reinforcing the ‘developmental origins of health and disease’ hypothesis. The boundary at the interface between biological and digital systems is becoming increasingly blurred, creating fertile ground for the integration of bioelectronics and artificial intelligence. Stark et al. (2026) review the development of flexible, implantable systems that utilise real-time data processing to close the loop between sensing and therapeutic intervention. These autonomous, closed-loop bioelectronic platforms are capable of sensing and modulating cardiac function in real time and represent an advancement in translational computational physiology. Technological progress in this area is supported by the development of sophisticated cellular-level models, such as the voltage-modulated Markov chain model for the Piezo1 ion channel presented by Ogiermann et al. (2026). By refining our ability to simulate mechanotransduction, such models provide the biophysical foundation for the next generation of intelligent, mechanosensitive devices. Furthermore, Greiner et al. (2025) introduce a deep learning-enabled toolkit for the automated 3D segmentation of ventricular cardiomyocytes to build these models at scale, effectively removing the manual bottleneck in high-resolution structural reconstruction. Physiological principles are tested and validated further at the extremes of environmental stress. Tripoli et al. (2026) used multiscale models to assess cardiac function across the spectrum from microgravity to hypergravity, informing spaceflight medicine and our fundamental understanding of gravitational adaptation. As Collia et al. (2026) note in an accompanying Perspective, these simulations allow us to explore physiological states that cannot be replicated in standard laboratory settings, offering a unique perspective on the robustness of cardiac function. Finally, comparative physiology continues to provide essential evolutionary benchmarks for identifying conserved mechanisms of cardiac automaticity and regeneration. This collection includes a novel mathematical model of the zebrafish ventricular action potential by Cestariolo et al. (2026), delivering a quantitative framework for this increasingly popular model of cardiac development. In the sinoatrial node, Linder et al. (2026) examine how sympathetic stimulation can compensate for hypocalcaemia-induced bradycardia. By comparing human and rabbit cells, they identify fundamental mechanisms of pacemaking robustness that are conserved across species as well as noable differences, thereby further grounding our computational reconstructions in comparative biological reality. The reliability of any computational model is inherently linked to the quality and resolution of the experimental data used for its construction and validation. As the complexity of simulations increases, so too does the requirement for highly accurate ground-truth datasets for parametrisation and validation. This collection showcases significant advancements in integrated experimental setups that allow for the comprehensive analysis of cardiac function across scales. Siles et al. (2026) describe a sophisticated platform for integrated panoramic optical and electrical mapping, enabling detailed arrhythmia studies in isolated hearts with high spatial and temporal resolution. This approach provides the necessary validation data for complex 3D ventricular models, a topic expanded upon by Zgierski-Johnston & Huisken (2025). They discuss the profound implications of being able to ‘see’ the heart in 3D in order to analyse the mechanical and electrical drivers of arrhythmia. However, even the most advanced experimental data are subject to variability and measurement error. Acknowledging that data is never ‘perfect’, Tanner et al. (2026) present a rigorous framework for uncertainty quantification in cardiac modelling. Focusing specifically on myocardial fibre orientation and activation patterns, they argue that incorporating error bars into simulation predictions is not simply a statistical exercise but a fundamental requirement for establishing the reliability and clinical safety of in silico results. By treating parameter uncertainty with the same rigour as experimental error, the research community can move towards more robust, reproducible, and ultimately translatable predictive models. The papers collected in this Special Issue demonstrate that computational physiology has evolved from a niche subdiscipline into a central pillar of modern cardiovascular research. The ability to integrate experimental data and computational simulations, spanning the scale from the molecular energetics of a crossbridge to the complex haemodynamic flows of a fibrillating atrium, is now a reality, not a distant aspiration. This convergence is accelerating the translation of fundamental physiological principles into clinical understanding and, ultimately, novel therapeutic strategies. As we look to the future, several key themes have emerged as priorities for the community. The first of these is the continued refinement and precise application of digital twin technology. While patient-specific modelling shows great potential for precision medicine, the contributions in this issue remind us that digital twins of in vitro experiments and generic mechanistic models, which explain fundamental biological principles, remain indispensable for interpreting experimental variability and defining the evolutionary robustness of cardiac function. Secondly, high-resolution, multi-modal experimental optical, ultrasound, and structural mapping must remain at the core of our field. This collection highlights that the next generation of in silico tools will only be as robust as the experimental data used to constrain them. Additionally, as our models move closer to the bedside, a rigorous commitment to uncertainty quantification and the inclusion of biological diversity, including age, sex, and developmental origins, is no longer optional; it is a key requirement for clinical safety and efficacy. Furthermore, the integration of machine learning and bioelectronics is enhancing our ability to reconstruct cardiac structure and modulate function in real time. The adoption of automated image analysis and autonomous closed-loop systems represents a practical shift towards more responsive physiological monitoring that remains grounded in the biophysical reality of the heart as a complex multicellular network. We hope that this Special Issue serves as both a comprehensive resource for the current community and a catalyst for the next generation of researchers. By continuing to close the gap between the analogue world and its digital representation, we can ensure that cardiac physiology remains at the forefront of the quest to understand, diagnose, treat, and ultimately prevent cardiovascular disease. abstract: April 2026: Top 10 Downloaded Article in Informatics Engineering, An International Journal (IEIJ) Informatics Engineering, An International Journal (IEIJ) http://airccse.org/journal/ieij/index.html ISSN: 2349 - 2198 FITTED OPERATOR FINITE DIFFERENCE METHOD FOR SINGULARLY PERTURBED PARABOLIC CONVECTIONDIFFUSION TYPE Tesfaye Aga Bullo and Gemechis File Duressa Department of Mathematics, College of Natural Science, Jimma University, Jimma, P.O. Box 378, Ethiopia ABSTRACT In this paper, we study the numerical solution of singularly perturbed parabolic convectiondiffusion type with boundary layers at the right side. To solve this problem, the backward-Euler with Richardson extrapolation method is applied on the time direction and the fitted operator finite difference method on the spatial direction is used, on the uniform grids. The stability and consistency of the method were established very well to guarantee the convergence of the method. Numerical experimentation is carried out on model examples, and the results are presented both in tables and graphs. Further, the present method gives a more accurate solution than some existing methods reported in the literature. Sharmila2 1 Assistant Professor, Department of Computer Science, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 2 Research Scholar, Department of Computer Science, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore ABSTRACT Big data is a prominent term which characterizes the improvement and availability of data in all three formats like structure, unstructured and semi formats. Structure data is located in a fixed field of a record or file and it is present in the relational data bases and spreadsheets whereas an unstructured data file includes text and multimedia contents. The primary objective of this big data concept is to describe the extreme volume of data sets i.e. both structured and unstructured. It is further defined with three “V” dimensions namely Volume, Velocity and Variety, and two more “V” also added i.e. Value and Veracity. Volume denotes the size of data, Velocity depends upon the speed of the data processing, Variety is described with the types of the data, Value which derives the business value and Veracity describes about the quality of the data and data understandability. Nowadays, big data has become unique and preferred research areas in the field of computer science. Many open research problems are available in big data and good solutions also been proposed by the researchers even though there is a need for development of many new techniques and algorithms for big data analysis in order to get optimal solutions. In this paper, a detailed study about big data, its basic concepts, history, applications, technique, research issues and tools are discussed.
