Keywords: School Information System (SIS); Fake Enrollments; Ghost Schools; Punjab; Pakistan

title: 00 | INTEGRATED STRATEGIES FOR PLASTIC MANAGEMENT: MONITORING, BIOLOGICAL IMPACT, AND CIRCULAR PERSPECTIVES ROUND TABLE REPORT – III PLASTAMINATION CONGRESS - 13 FEBRUARY 2026
authors: Società Italiana di Biologia Sperimentale
venue: Journal of Biological Research - Bollettino della Società Italiana di Biologia Sperimentale
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; News Media
Tags: Environmental Protection; Marine Strategy Framework; Circular Economy; Policy Development; Public Awareness
abstract: Introduction The PLASTAMINATION Congress held on 13 February 2026 at the University of Palermo, convened a multidisciplinary panel of experts to address the escalating challenge of micro- and nanoplastic (MNP) contamination. Characterized during the session as a silent invasion MNP pollution represents a systemic threat that bridges environmental degradation and human pathology. This round table synthesized perspectives on the entire plastic lifecycle: from urban point sources and regional monitoring to ecotoxicological risks and advanced materials engineering. The discussion underscored that the traditional conceptual boundary between the environment and the human organism is no longer sustainable, necessitating a coordinated scientific and institutional response grounded in the One Health framework. Science communication and public responsibility The round table opened with a reflection on the role of science communication and public responsibility, moderated by Giorgia Görner Enrile, journalist for SciliaMedica.it and ilSicilia.it, who framed the discussion around the relationship between scientific knowledge, information, and society. Communicating health today means more than sharing scientific results, because communication itself carries an ethical responsibility toward society. The III PLASTAMINATION Conference 2026 and the project PLASTAMINATION Poly (Lactic Acid) plastics contamination therefore address not only the issue of plastic and MNP contamination, but also the way scientific evidence reaches the public and the effects it may generate in terms of trust, fear, or awareness. The One Health perspective reminds us that environmental, animal, and human health remain deeply interconnected, but it also highlights the responsibility involved in communicating these topics, since scientific data and research findings can easily enter public debate and sometimes become instrumentalized within environmental, social, or political narratives. Public discussion does not necessarily represent a problem, but difficulties arise when the complexity of scientific research becomes reduced to simplified messages or slogans. This dynamic becomes even more evident within the contemporary information ecosystem, where media attention often focuses on headlines rather than on scientific context, while information increasingly circulates through social networks whose visibility mechanisms depend more on algorithms than on the reliability of sources. In this environment, communication about health and environmental risks should not only inform but also encourage critical thinking, helping people recognize reliable information and distinguish between evidence, interpretation, and sensationalism. Transparency therefore plays a crucial role in scientific and institutional communication, but transparency does not mean oversimplification or reassurance at any cost, since trust grows from clarity and from the recognition of the limits that accompany scientific knowledge. Environmental monitoring and infrastructure vulnerabilities Effective mitigation of marine plastic pollution begins with robust environmental surveillance and standardized monitoring programmes. Dr. Salvatore Campanella and Dr. Alessandro Aglialoro, ARPA Sicilia, presented the environmental monitoring activities carried out by the regional agency within the framework of the Marine Strategy Framework Directive (MSFD, Directive 2008/56/EC). This European policy instrument aims to achieve and maintain the Good Environmental Status (GES) of marine waters through coordinated monitoring systems implemented by Member States and supported in Italy by the Ministry for the Environment, ISPRA, and the National System for Environmental Protection (SNPA). Within this framework, ARPA Sicilia conducts periodic monitoring campaigns in coastal and offshore waters using standardized sampling protocols. These surveys include the collection and analysis of seawater, marine sediments, and biological matrices (biota) in order to assess environmental quality and detect the presence of anthropogenic contaminants. Monitoring activities also address the distribution of marine litter and microplastics, including the assessment of floating debris, beached litter, and microplastic particles in the water column and sediments, as required by the indicators of Descriptor 10 (Marine Litter) of the Marine Strategy. The data generated through these monitoring programmes contribute to national and European environmental reporting systems and support the scientific assessment of the ecological status of the Mediterranean Sea. These datasets provide an essential basis for environmental policy, risk assessment, and the development of mitigation strategies at both national and European levels. In addition to monitoring activities, ARPA Sicilia participates in international scientific cooperation initiatives and promotes environmental education and awareness programmes, including outreach activities aimed at schools and local communities, with the objective of increasing public understanding of marine pollution and fostering sustainable environmental practices. However, the urban origin of plastic contaminants remains a critical factor in their transfer to aquatic environments. Prof. Michele Torregrossa (University of Palermo) highlighted the dual role of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) in the environmental dynamics of microplastic pollution. On the one hand, conventional wastewater treatment processes, including primary sedimentation, biological treatment, and secondary clarification, together with advanced technologies such as membrane bioreactors (MBRs), are capable of removing a substantial fraction of microplastics from treated effluents. Several studies report removal efficiencies frequently exceeding 90% for larger microplastic particles, although efficiency depends on particle size and plant configuration. On the other hand, WWTPs act as accumulation points for microplastics, since most of the particles retained during treatment are transferred to sewage sludge. This creates a secondary environmental concern, particularly when sludge is reused in agriculture or disposed of in landfills, potentially reintroducing microplastics into terrestrial ecosystems. The situation may be further exacerbated by extreme weather events associated with climate change. During intense rainfall, combined sewer systems may generate Combined Sewer Overflows (CSOs), temporarily bypassing wastewater treatment plants and discharging untreated wastewater directly into receiving water bodies. Under these conditions, several studies have reported that microplastic concentrations in urban runoff and CSO discharges can be up to 5 or 10 times higher than those measured in treated wastewater effluents. This increase is mainly related to the wash-off of urban surfaces, the mobilization of plastic particles accumulated within sewer networks, and the resuspension of sediments during high-flow conditions. These findings highlight the need for integrated management strategies addressing both urban wastewater infrastructure and plastic pollution mitigation, in order to reduce the transfer of microplastics from urban systems to aquatic environments. Biological risks From Ecotoxicology to Human Health The transition of plastics from environmental matrices to biological tissues was a central theme. Prof. Federica Scalia (UniKore) presented evidence regarding the invisible risks of biodegradable polymers, specifically Polylactic Acid (PLA). Utilizing the zebrafish model, her research demonstrated that PLA nanoplastics can bioaccumulate in zebrafish, especially in the gastrointestinal system, within 120 hours post-fertilization. These exposures were linked to significant gene expression alterations, specifically in cellular stress markers, suggesting that eco-friendly alternatives require rigorous toxicological scrutiny during embryonic development. These findings resonate with the human health perspectives shared by Prof. Stefania D’Angelo (Parthenope University of Naples). MNPs have shifted from being external contaminants to internal systemic constituents, detected in the lungs, liver, placenta, and brain. The discussion detailed how MNPs trigger oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, and the disruption of critical biological barriers. Of particular clinical concern is the presence of MNPs within atherosclerotic plaques, which is increasingly associated with a higher risk of major cardiovascular events, and their potential role in neuroinflammatory processes related to dementia. Circular solutions and materials science To counter these threats, the panel explored resilient technological pathways. Prof. Annamaria Visco (University of Messina), principal investigator of the European LIFE RESTART project, presented an applied model of circular economy in the field of polymeric materials, based on the valorization of agro-industrial by-products and their integration into biodegradable polymer matrices. The project, funded under the LIFE Programme of the European Union, aims to develop sustainable composite materials obtained from residual biomass derived from agricultural and agro-industrial supply chains, contributing to the reduction of fossil-based raw materials and promoting new circular production models. The research activities carried out by the University of Messina research group have led to the development of polymeric biocomposites obtained by incorporating plant residues and agro-industrial by-products into biodegradable matrices. This approach allows the valorization of organic waste streams, transforming them into high-value materials, while simultaneously reducing the environmental impact associated with conventional plastic production. A key element of the project is the establishment of the “Fabbrica Zero” pilot plant in Roccavaldina (Messina), a demonstration facility designed to transfer laboratory research results to an industrial scale. Within this framework, materials developed in academic laboratories are processed through technologies compatible with existing industrial transformation processes, demonstrating the technical feasibility of producing biocomposites from residual biomass. During the discussion, the economic dimension also clearly emerged as a critical factor influencing the large-scale adoption of these materials. The development and production costs of sustainable biocomposites are currently higher than those of conventional fossil-based plastics, particularly during the early phases of technological deployment. As a result, many companies face significant challenges in independently sustaining the transition toward more sustainable materials. It was therefore highlighted that a structural transformation of production systems requires not only scientific innovation but also targeted policy measures and economic incentives capable of supporting companies in adopting environmentally sustainable materials and processes. In this context, Prof. Andrea Pace (University of Palermo) emphasized that the development of new materials must be framed within a broader perspective integrating scientific research, industrial policies, and innovation governance. According to Pace, the transition toward more sustainable polymeric materials cannot rely solely on technological progress but requires the creation of innovation ecosystems in which universities, research centers, industries, and public institutions collaborate in a coordinated and structured manner. It emerged that strengthening technology transfer mechanisms, supporting applied research, and developing dedicated funding instruments could play a crucial role in accelerating the integration of sustainable materials into industrial systems. In this perspective, policy tools such as incentives for companies adopting low-impact materials, green public procurement strategies, and innovation programmes promoted at the European level could significantly reduce the economic barriers that still limit the widespread adoption of these technologies. Finally, Dr. Dario Scalia, Plastic Free Onlus highlighted the crucial role of civil society in addressing plastic pollution. Alongside technological solutions and industrial policies, environmental volunteering initiatives, awareness campaigns, and territorial clean-up activities represent concrete tools to reduce plastic dispersion in natural ecosystems and to promote collective responsibility in environmental protection. During the discussion, the importance of environmental education targeting younger generations was also strongly emphasized. Educational programmes in schools, awareness initiatives, and participatory activities involving students represent key instruments for fostering a culture of sustainability from an early age. It emerged that engaging children and young people in environmental education initiatives can play a fundamental role in shaping long-term behavioral change and in promoting responsible attitudes toward environmental protection. Conclusion The PLASTAMINATION round table concluded that the fight against plastic contamination requires an integrated, multi-scalar approach. Enhancing the filtration capacity of WWTPs is insufficient if the resulting sludge is not managed or if materials are not designed for sustainability from their inception. The detection of epigenetic impacts and the systemic accumulation of plastics in vital human organs mandate a paradigm shift: plastic is no longer merely a waste management issue but a critical determinant of global public health.
Keywords: Plastic Pollution; Microplastics; Environmental Monitoring; Bioaccumulation; Public Health; One Health; Sustainable Materials

title: Building a Digital Media Platform for the European Public Sphere
authors: Christoph Neuberger
Tags: Digital Sovereignty; Common Good; Platform Design; Public Value
abstract: Introduction The currently dominant digital platforms are associated with many negative effects on democratic societies, such as disinformation, hate speech, and propaganda. In the case of X, the platform has become a personal megaphone for its owner Elon Musk. The “Great Sysop” has gained considerable power over public opinion formation and is promoting “platform illiberalism” (Magalhães et al.). However, Twitter has also added valuable features that have enhanced public communication, particulary in journalism, which is the focus of the present article. Journalists used Twitter for purposes such as live reporting, news aggregation, including citizens as sources, organising debates (Artwick), and fact checking (Coddington et al.). Hermida coined the term “ambient journalism” to describe journalism’s role on Twitter as a novel “awareness system”. In 2014, a newsroom survey in Germany compared the use of different platforms in journalism (Neuberger et al.). Twitter was most often used for brief breaking news and real-time reporting. Editorial staff also utilised Twitter and Facebook relatively often for investigation and monitoring. Twitter was used especially to contact elite sources, such as experts and prominent people, and for monitoring their public appearances. Twitter and Facebook were the preferred platforms for user participation in journalism. Twitter served as a testing ground for new forms of journalism. But this is all history. These achievements have been lost since Elon Musk took over the platform. This raises the questions: how should a common good-oriented platform be designed and governed? What lessons can be learned from the journalistic adaptation of Twitter? I will address these questions by focussing on the transnational European public sphere. European Public Sphere as Goal The philosopher Jürgen Habermas (Für Europa), who passed away in March 2026, continued to advocate for European autonomy, integration, and democratisation, particularly in the context of today's polycrisis. He believed that establishing a common European public sphere by transnationalising the many national public spheres is essential (Habermas, Demokratie 188–91). But the European public sphere still has significant shortcomings (e.g., Brüggemann and Kleinen-von Königslöw; Brüggemann et al.). There is a lack of European media, audiences interested in European issues, and European perspectives in national media. Europeanisation is particularly evident in the vertical dimension (“looking towards Brussels”), but less so in the horizontal dimension. Looking beyond national borders at other national discourses is therefore crucial here. Another weakness is the low citizen participation in the European public sphere. The question of a European public sphere has regained importance in recent years. In light of the current geopolitical situation, the European Union (EU) is striving for greater digital sovereignty. This concept emphasises state authority over the Internet, replacing the earlier concept of “multistakeholderism”, which viewed the Internet as a distinct, decentralised, and transnational space beyond the reach of politics (Pohle and Santianello). The call for digital sovereignty encompasses not only the technical and economic dimension, but also the political and cultural dimension. Digital sovereignty “must not be conflated with isolation or protectionism, in the European context, it can be described as the capacity of being independent while remaining open to collaboration and committed to shared values such as transparency, democracy, and the rule of law” (Bria et al. 10). European values are often cited as the goal of digital sovereignty (e.g., Di Marco et al.; Kagermann and Wilhelm; Roberts et al.), but are not precisely defined. The EU is often criticised for over-regulating platforms and AI, hindering innovation and competitiveness. However, there are serious doubts as to whether this criticism is valid (Bradford, False Choice). Nevertheless, the EU must not only regulate, but also provide better platforms. This requires a change in mindset: regulation only defines the minimum normative requirements that must be met in every case. This negative approach must then be complemented by a positive one that describes how platforms can promote the common good. The EU could offer a more appealing alternative to the libertarian approach of the US and the authoritarian approach of China (Bradford, Digital Empires). Public Sphere and Public Value But how can the common good be defined? In the media sector, it is usually referred to as “public value”. This management concept was introduced by Mark H. Moore in his seminal book Creating Public Value. It was intended as a practical framework for public sector management, offering an alternative to the market-oriented, neo-liberal “new public management” and its premise that the public good is simply the sum of individual consumer interests. In contrast to this market-oriented view, the public value concept focusses on contributions to society’s common good beyond individual self-interest (Alford and Hughes 132; Moore, “Reflections” 366–9). The BBC adopted it in its programmatic statement Building Public Value. In this statement, public value was defined through three “public principles” (universality, fairness and equity, accountability) and five “public purposes” (democratic value, cultural and creative value, educational value, social and community value, global value) (7–8). A recent paper, Creating and Measuring Dynamic Public Value at the BBC, distinguishes between individual value, social value, and industrial value (Mazzucato et al. 23–8). The BBC’s public value test for public broadcasting outlets has been adopted by many EU countries (Collins; Donders). Some scholars have criticised a lack of clarity about what public value means substantially (e.g., Benington 42–6; Collins 175). Others have argued that public value cannot be defined “in some absolute sense” (Alford and Hughes 133) because public value must be, at least partially, open to the outcomes of democratic political processes (Alford and O’Flynn 175–8; Benington 233–4). The concept of hybrid common good offers a solution for these contradictory expectations (Blum; Neuberger, Auftrag). According to this approach, the common good comprises a substantive component consisting of liberal-democratic values to be realised in the value creation process, alongside public discourse as procedural component, in which those values are interpreted and concretised. These values are the “guardrails”, setting the boundaries for possible outcomes of the democratic public discourse, and also the standards for the discourse itself. How can the European values be defined more precisely? One obvious option is to derive them from normative theories of democracy. Several proposals have been made as to how normative theories of democracy can be applied to the public sphere and journalism (e.g., Christians et al. 95–105, 125–7; Ferree et al.; Gerhards; Martinsen; McQuail; Strömbäck). While these typologies differ, the theories agree that the media should at least create a common public sphere and a shared body of knowledge on current topics of public interest. Both are necessary prerequisites for the formation of individual and collective opinion. Valid and shared knowledge requires news production to be based on journalistic standards and to allow freedom of information and equal access to knowledge. However, beyond this, the theories make different demands regarding the process of opinion formation. Deliberative theory sets higher normative expectations for discourse progression than liberal and participatory theory (Martinsen). It demands a rational, respectful, and domination-free discourse in which the persuasiveness of the better argument—in the best case—leads to consensus. In summary, nine values of the public sphere can be derived from theories of democracy, whose normative expectations partially overlap: news and discourse quality, freedom, equality, diversity, power symmetry, criticism and control, security, and integration (Neuberger, Öffentlichkeit). Initiatives to Build a European Media Platform A more democratic digital public sphere cannot be achieved simply by regulating existing platform and AI companies. It is equally important to create media platforms and AI applications that are oriented towards the common good as an alternative to the commercial offerings that currently dominate the market. Although Habermas acknowledged the potential of the digital public sphere to contribute to the emancipation of citizens, he viewed the Internet as an “ambivalent explosive force” (Habermas, New Structural 35). He missed a “substitute for the professional selection and discursive examination of contents based on accepted cognitive standards” (38–39) on these platforms. Since 2017, there has been an ongoing discussion about the idea of a European media platform oriented towards the common good that would fulfil European values, create a European public sphere, and strengthen Europe’s digital sovereignty. Ulrich Wilhelm, the director of the Bavarian Broadcasting Corporation (Bayerischer Rundfunk), was the first and strongest advocate of this idea. He expressed concern that “Europe is in danger of losing digital sovereignty over its defining values” (Wilhelm 15; translated by the author). Van Dijck, Poell, and de Waal (146) outlined a “value-centric design of the platform society”, which should realise “public values” such as safety, privacy, transparency, accuracy, democratic control, quality, accessibility, comprehensiveness, equality, inclusiveness, fair treatment, responsibility, and accountability (139–41). Hillje (133–60) proposed four elements for a “Platform Europe”: a European newsroom and a European news service, opportunities for citizens to participate, a cultural connection representing Europe's diversity and common “European Way of Life”, and apps for citizens to take action and interact. Another plan proposed the creation of a European infrastructure for public service media and other media, as well as for science, culture, and citizens (Kagermann and Wilhelm). What has been achieved until now? Not much. Several proposals have been made to create a European media platform. The Council for European Public Space, an NGO, has developed a draft for a decentralised platform for trustworthy news called “Delphi” (Pfeffer). The Public Spaces Incubator (PSI) brings together public service media from different countries to work together to develop platforms for better public dialogue. The PSI toolkit will be made publicly available as open-source software by 2027. The European Cultural Foundation develops and supports cultural initiatives that foster “belonging, solidarity, and democratic values—building connections across Europe and empowering communities to shape a shared future.” It presented the draft for European Digital Public Spaces back in 2020. France and Germany intend to expand their joint cultural television channel arte into a European media platform (Der Beauftragte der Bundesregierung für Kultur und Medien). In 2025, the Second German Television (ZDF) presented its plan for a public-interest-oriented Digital Public Open Space (Dogruel et al.). There are many plans, but it remains to be seen whether they can be successfully realised in future. The drafts for a European media platform largely leave open the question of which values they intend to achieve and how. Only smaller projects promoting European news exchange and discourse have been established so far. One such project is the press review euro|topics – European Press Roundup, which monitors European discourses and translates the main contributions from leading newspapers into five languages. This project was founded by the German Federal Agency for Civic Education (Bundeszentrale für politische Bildung, bpb) as early as 2005. A European Perspective (AEP) is a transnational European newsroom, launched by public service media in 2021. Its 23 member organisations from 17 countries exchange news and translate news using the AI-assisted EuroVox tool. In 2024, AEP launched YEP News (Your European Perspective), a multilingual news aggregator that reports on major European and global issues. Outline for a Common Good-Oriented European Platform for Journalism A compelling concept for such a platform is still lacking. While Twitter certainly opened up new possibilities for journalism, it was not primarily created to improve democracy. Therefore, simply copying it would not suffice. It would be better to first define the modules required for a European media platform that is oriented towards the common good. In line with the mission of public service media, such a platform could encompass journalism, education, advice, culture, and entertainment. This article, however, focusses on journalism, which contributes to the common good in two ways: by producing and disseminating news and comments, and by moderating public discourse. Unlike traditional mass media, digital platforms are not limited to the linear dissemination of news and comments. They also enable interactive communication, broad participation in the public discourse, and the archiving of verified news and justified opinions. Taking these digital capabilities into account, a matrix of journalistic platform modules can be developed that integrates dissemination, discourse, and archiving (table 1). These modules complement each other and are closely interconnected: news and comments distributed by the media provide ongoing opportunities for public discourse, during which their justification is questioned and clarified through argumentation. Validated news and opinions, along with their justifications, are stored in the archive and can be reintroduced into the process, serving as context for current events or providing argumentative support. However, they can also be corrected and supplemented themselves. Knowledge Assessment Journalistic intermediation Short-term: Production and dissemination News Comment Professional production and linear dissemination Medium-term: Public discourse Epistemic validation Opinion formation process Professional moderation (ensuring broad participation and deliberative quality of discourses) Long-term: Archiving Validated knowledge as context Documentation of opinion and its justification (pros and cons) Professional archiving and referencing Table 1: Modules of a journalistic platform The envisioned journalistic platform modules should connect three spatial levels: the local, national, and European. Yochai Benkler’s normative concept of an integrated networked public sphere, which he outlined back in 2006, is helpful here. The goal of this concept is to strike the right balance between openness and closedness in public discourse. Both are necessary, but also risky: openness brings participation and diversity, but also information overload, loss of news quality (disinformation), and rude discourse (hate speech). On the side of closedness, gatekeeping ensures news and discourse quality, but focussing attention on a small number of relevant topics (agenda-setting) can lead to a concentration of power and lack of diversity. According to Benkler, the tension between openness and closedness should be resolved by distributing specific tasks across the different levels of the public sphere and by the gradual processing of news and comments in different phases. This begins with a high degree of diversity in topics and related news and comments across numerous mini-publics at the local level, with strong citizen participation. This continues at the national and European level, mediated by journalistic selection and moderation. At the European level, exchanges between the national publics should be encouraged. At higher levels and in later phases, the publics merge and diversity in rational discourse is reduced when selection decisions are made about the relevance of topics, the accuracy of news, and the (better) justification of opinions. This narrowing of the focus in a bottom-up process should not be centralised or finalised. Rather than the “hard” selection decisions (gatekeeping) of traditional mass media, a recommendation-based approach and “soft” (revisable) selection process (gatewatching: see Bruns) should be adopted. This multi-level architecture helps to ensure that the concrete local experiences of citizens remain connected to the political decisions made at higher levels in distant capitals. Topics can be covered and discussed on platforms in the long term, with knowledge and opinions from the archive providing context. Of course, theories of democracy should not be the only normative benchmark for the European media platform. Additional modules are required to strengthen identity, community building, social cohesion, and connections between different lifeworld experiences (Bengesser). Steps Towards a Common Good-Oriented European Media Platform What steps are necessary for the design of such a European media platform? Digital transformation is an ongoing process of change, meaning that the conditions for realising democratic values are also perpetually changing (Coeckelbergh, Digital Humanism 3). This requires continuous learning and weighing up of new chances and risks. Appropriate learning environments include innovation labs (García-Avilés) and pioneer communities (Hepp and Loosen). This process of experimentation could be observed in the case of Twitter and is also currently evident in the field of generative artificial intelligence (Jungherr). Negative effects have become apparent, such as incorrect outputs, bias, unfairness, opacity, copyright violations, and environmental costs. Conversely, visions were developed on how to strengthen democracy with AI and how to democratise AI (Coeckelbergh, Undermines 70-119). There are already many AI applications that are conducive to democracy: LLM-powered chatbots can enhance news diversity (Heitz et al.), improve the quality of political discourse (Argyle et al.), and verify political information (Kuznetsova et al.). Pioneer communities such as the German AI for Media Network support systematic learning. An experimental approach is needed to exploit the opportunities and limits of digital technologies for democracy, following the concepts of “experimental democracy” by John Dewey and “piecemeal technology” by Karl R. Popper. Both authors recommended taking small, reversible steps to test new paths in democracy. In addition to the editorial development of a European media platform outlined here, the necessary institutional and legal frameworks must be created. If a new organisation is not established, existing public service media organisations should cooperate within the framework of the European Broadcasting Union (EBU), for example. Furthermore, a political consensus must be reached among EU member states. This will certainly be the greatest challenge. The rise of right-wing populist parties and the erosion of democracy in several EU countries is both the main reason for taking measures to strengthen the European public sphere, but also the biggest obstacle. Given this challenging situation, the only feasible approach is a gradual rollout of a European media platform, starting with a small number of countries and focussing on verified news as minimum consensus. The hope must be that the creation of a European public sphere will raise democratic awareness and promote European solidarity. Digital platforms can be useful in this respect; Twitter has demonstrated this potential early on. References Alford, John, and Janine O’Flynn. “Making Sense of Public Value: Concepts, Critiques and Emergent Meanings.” International Journal of Public Administration 32 (2009): 171–91. Alford, John, and Owen Hughes. “Public Value Pragmatism as the Next Phase of Public Management. The American Review of Public Administration 38 (2008): 130–48. Argyle, Lisa P., Christopher A. Artwick, Claudette G. “Reporters on Twitter: Product or Service?” Digital Journalism 1 (2013): 212–28. BBC. Building Public Value: Renewing the BBC for a Digital World. London: BBC, 2004. Bengesser, Cathrin. “Public Value after the News: A Lifeworld-Based Approach to the Legitimation of Content Universality as Contribution to Society.” In Public Service Media’s Contribution to Society: RIPE@2021. Eds. Manuel Puppis and Christopher Ali. Gothenburg: Nordicom, University of Gothenburg, 2023. 47–68. Benington, John. “From Private Choice to Public Value?” In Public Value: Theory and Practice. Eds. John Benington and Mark Harrison Moore. Basingstoke: Palgrave Macmillan, 2011. 31–51. Benkler, Yochai. The Wealth of Networks: How Social Production Transforms Markets and Freedom. title: Urban economics for sustainable transitions: grand challenges in urban structure, the urban–rural gradient and city systems
authors: Julie Le Gallo
Tags: urban structure; land markets; environmental quality; governance; spatial organization
abstract: The world is now irreversibly urban. More than half of humanity already lives in urban areas, and United Nations projections indicate that roughly two thirds of world population growth between now and 2050 will occur in cities, with the vast majority of new urban dwellers concentrated not only in global megacities, but also in small and medium-sized cities across sub-Saharan Africa and Central and Southern Asia (United Nations, 2025). At the same time, significant parts of the urban systems of East-Central Europe, Japan, and the former industrial heartlands of North America are experiencing population decline. The twenty-first-century city is, therefore, in the same moment rapidly growing and quietly shrinking, depending on where one looks within the global urban system and within countries themselves.These twin dynamics of expansion and contraction cannot be adequately understood from any vantage point other than the city itself. First, the city is a space organized around gradients of densities and land values, structured by agglomeration forces and the friction of distance. As such, it is an economic object, whose internal spatial logic generates dynamics that are irreducible to other scales. Second, it is a node in a wider system of cities, connected by flows of people, firms, capital, and knowledge whose structure shapes which cities grow, which stagnate, and which decline. Third, it is a political and institutional arena where collective choices about land use, mobility, and environmental quality are made by actors (municipal governments, planning agencies, and elected officials) with both a democratic mandate and operational authority over the built environment. These properties generate specific economic dynamics that operate very differently across the urban size distribution.At one extreme, megacities and global cities function as hubs in international networks of finance, producer services, talent, and innovation (Sassen, 1991), concentrating connectivity in ways that can simultaneously stimulate and drain the national urban systems in which they are embedded. At the other, intermediate cities and rural towns occupy structurally distinct positions along the urban-rural gradient, with more limited access to agglomeration externalities and asymmetric exposure to the costs of sustainability transitions. How these dynamics play out across the full city system, from globally connected metropolis to intermediate town to rural periphery, and through which governance mechanisms they are shaped, is at the heart of this research agenda. Indeed, it is precisely because the city concentrates people, activities, and infrastructure in space that it is the primary site through which sustainability transitions are both shaped and experienced. Urban form determines commuting distances and thus transport emissions. Land markets govern who can live close to green amenities, jobs, or flood-safe ground. The position of a city in its national hierarchy, and possibly its international exposure, conditions whether the gains from a green industrial transition accrue locally or are captured elsewhere. Cities are, furthermore, the scale at which most sustainability commitments are most directly translated into enforceable spatial decisions, through zoning, transport investment, building codes, and environmental standards. This makes urban governance both a driver and a battleground of the sustainability transition.A thread of distributional concern runs through each of these dimensions and is made explicit in each of the grand challenges we identify. Within cities, the spatial organization of land markets and the location of public investment determine who captures the benefits of urban sustainability and who is displaced by rising land values. Along the urban-rural gradient, the incidence of environmental pricing instruments and planning regulations falls unequally across space and income groups, in ways that are increasingly visible in the political mobilization of peripheral populations. Across city systems, the architecture of urban hierarchies conditions whether the productivity and fiscal gains from sustainability transitions diffuse to intermediate cities and rural regions or concentrate in a small number of globally connected metropolises.Urban economics is therefore also an economics of spatial justice, and this section invites research that makes these distributional stakes analytically explicit.For all these reasons, urban economics, understood as the economics of how cities are internally organized and how they relate to one another within national and international systems, is not peripheral to the sustainability agenda: it is central to it. This Specialty Grand Challenge paper sets out the research priorities for the Urban Economics section of Frontiers in Sustainable Cities. Building on the foundational editorial by Griffith (2021), which established the importance of geography and spatial dependence for this section, we first clarify what makes a space economically urban by framing urban economics around the urban-rural gradient as a conceptual foundation for the section. Building on this, we the articulate three interconnected grand challenges that will guide the section's development: (1) urban structure, land markets, and the distributional stakes of sustainability within cities; (2) the distributional effects of urban sustainability policies along the urban-rural gradient; and (3) city systems, urban hierarchies, and the spatial equity of sustainable growth across national territory. We also articulate the methodological standards, anchored in causal inference, quantitative spatial models, and modern machine learning, that the section will uphold, and make an explicit call for geographic diversity in empirical contributions.Urban economics rests on a deceptively simple observation: proximity matters. When economic agents (workers, firms, households) locate close to one another, the costs of interaction fall and productive externalities emerge. This core insight, formalized in the monocentric city model (Fujita, 1989) and later generalized for polycentric cities, generates a gradient: densities, land values, wages, and access to amenities all vary systematically with distance from economic centers. Consequently, the urban-rural gradient is not a binary classification but a continuum, shaped by the interplay of agglomeration economies and the friction of distance.The practical implication is that the boundary between urban and rural is not fixed. Rather, it is endogenous to the economic forces that the field seeks to understand. Administrative definitions of urban areas, whether based on population thresholds, administrative status, or built-up land (Dijkstra et al., 2021), are necessary for measurement but insufficient as conceptual anchors. What matters economically is position along the gradient, which determines access to thick labor markets and dense networks of buyers, suppliers, and knowledge spillovers on one end; exposure to land-intensive production, longer commutes, and thinner markets on the other. Villages that lie within commuting range of large cities are functionally urban in many respects and intermediate cities that anchor regional labor markets play a structuring role in the organization of national space that may not appear when the study is focused on large metropolitan areas.A related dimension concerns the relational economics of urban-rural linkages. Cities and their hinterlands are bound together by flows of labor, food, capital, and ecological services. The growth of large cities reshapes the economic prospects of rural regions and intermediate towns, sometimes through dynamic agglomeration effects that generate broader regional prosperity, sometimes through extractive centralization that hollows out the periphery (Tacoli, 2003).These flows also have a governance dimension: the jurisdictional boundaries of metropolitan governments rarely coincide with the functional economic territory over which urban agglomeration effects operate, creating institutional fragmentation that conditions both the efficiency and the equity of urban-rural interactions.This gradient-based understanding clarifies the scope of urban economics as a discipline. A research question belongs to urban economics when the urban-rural gradient -its determinants, its spatial organization, its distributional consequences -is constitutive of the question itself.How agglomeration shapes productivity, how land use regulations affect housing affordability and residential sorting, how transport infrastructure reshapes access along the gradient, how environmental amenities are priced differently across urban and peri-urban contexts, how cities grow and decline within national and global urban hierarchies; these are questions for which the spatial structure of cities and the relations between cities are not incidental features of the data but the very mechanisms under investigation. Governance changes, such as reforms to zoning law, shifts in fiscal decentralization, extensions of metropolitan authority, and changes in the institutional boundary of urban jurisdictions, are integral to this agenda.The most immediate expression of the urban-rural gradient is the internal structure of the city itself, the way land is priced, density distributed, and accessibility unevenly allocated across neighborhoods. It is at this intra-urban scale that the first grand challenge is situated.The internal spatial organization of cities, how densely they develop, how land uses mix and separate, how accessible different locations are to jobs and services, is the most direct determinant of the sustainability of urban life. Urban form shapes energy consumption in buildings and transport, governs exposure to environmental hazards and amenities, and conditions the economic opportunities available to different households at different locations (Glaeser and Kahn, 2010). Yet, the causal relationships between urban structure and sustainability outcomes remain incompletely understood, in part because, empirically, the endogeneity of location choices and land use patterns poses severe identification challenges.Transit-oriented development (TOD) illustrates both the promise and the complexity. The colocation of high-density residential and commercial development around public transit nodes is among the most widely advocated urban strategies for reducing car dependence and the associated carbon footprint of urban mobility. A growing body of quasi-experimental work has begun to document the effects of transit investments on land values, modal choices, and development patterns in their immediate catchment areas (Gendron-Carrier et al., 2022;Dubé et al., 2025;Mann and Levinson, 2026). Extending this evidence base to cities at earlier stages of transit development, and to lower-income contexts where the substitution margin between private and public modes is very different, constitutes a key methodological frontier. Quasiexperimental designs exploiting staggered transit openings, geographic discontinuities around station catchment areas, or historically determined route alignments that are orthogonal to current demand pressures offer a productive path forward. The governance structure of transit planning, i.e. who controls investment decisions, how metropolitan authorities relate to national governments, and whether financing is centralized or devolved, also shapes the distributive reach of transit-led sustainability, and changes in institutional authority over transit financing constitute a natural quasi-experimental lever for identification.Housing markets are the key transmission mechanism through which urban structure shapes distributional outcomes. Affordability crises in high-demand cities are not simply the result of aggregate supply shortfalls: they reflect both the aggregate misallocation of labor across cities due to housing supply constraints (Hsieh and Moretti, 2019) and the spatial mismatch between housing affordability, employment location, and transport accessibility within metropolitan areas (Kain, 1968). The incidence of climate risk is similarly mediated by housing markets: flood risk, urban heat island exposure, and coastal erosion are increasingly capitalized in property prices (Bernstein et al., 2019), but the distribution of this capitalization across income groups and geographies raises important questions about who bears the long-run cost of climate adaptation in cities.Indeed, the sustainability transition in cities generates a profound tension around environmental justice precisely because green investments are mediated by housing markets. Parks, greenways, urban forests, flood-resilient infrastructure, raise the quality of life in targeted neighborhoods, but they also raise land values, triggering gentrification and potentially displacing the low-income residents they were meant to benefit. This phenomenon, documented under the terms 'green gentrification' or 'environmental gentrification' (Wolch et al., 2014;Rigolon and Németh, 2020), A growing body of work moves beyond reduced-form price effects to examine the mechanisms and actors that drive displacement, including the political economy of pro-growth urban coalitions and real estate interests and the sequencing of greening and gentrification (Rigolon and Collins, 2023). Yet, credible causal evidence that directly traces household displacement, as opposed to land value appreciation, remains scarce.Key open questions include the role of local housing supply regulations in mediating the translation of amenity gains into displacement pressure, and the extent to which institutional context, tenure security, zoning flexibility, municipal fiscal capacity, conditions whether greening triggers displacement or instead benefits incumbent residents. Governance variation across municipalities with different rent control regimes, social housing endowments, or inclusionary zoning requirements thus constitutes a valuable source of identification alongside hedonic price methods and difference-in-differences designs.More broadly, the political economy of land use regulation, including the conditions under which municipalities loosen restrictive zoning to allow densification, mixed-use development, and transit-adjacent housing supply, constitutes one of the most active and policy-relevant frontiers of contemporary urban economics (Gyourko and Molloy, is not a housing it is a sustainability whose to transport and residential access to employment and amenities on the spatial of and the of the private development that how through housing markets, labor markets, and urban exploiting variation across or the staggered of is a for this from the intra-urban to the the distributional stakes of sustainability not at the city they along the full from urban core to rural sustainability policies are rarely energy for and green zoning all costs and benefits that are unevenly across not only within cities, but along the urban-rural gradient, from central through peri-urban to rural areas structurally from the benefits of urban environmental The economic of these distributive effects is one of the most and challenges urban concern is the of environmental pricing carbon on and energy costs to a of the of low-income which are also more to live from employment on private and in housing they not and 2010). the spatial dimension a of complexity. title: The problem with polarization research in an age of extremism
authors: Felipe Vilanova; Flavio Azevedo
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; News Media; Public Records
Tags: political conflict; social psychology; behavioral science; democratic erosion; scientific inquiry
abstract: "I have been gravely disappointed with the white moderate. I have almost reached the regrettable conclusion that the Negro's great stumbling block in his stride toward freedom is not the White Citizen's Counciler or the Ku Klux Klanner, but the white moderate, who is more devoted to "order" than to justice; who prefers a negative peace which is the absence of tension to a positive peace which is the presence of justice"-Martin Luther King, Jr., Letter from Birmingham Jail (1963) Over the past decade, political polarization has become one of the most studied topics in the behavioral and social sciences. A search for "political polarization" in the Web of Science database (conducted on 23 October 2025) yielded 13,387 results, and more than 60 per cent of which were published since 2020. During the same period, authoritarian and anti-democratic movements have gained strength across diverse contexts. A Friedrich-Ebert-Stiftung (2023) study reported that support for a dictatorship led by a "strong leader" in Germany increased, and endorsement of social-Darwinist statements such as "some lives are worthless" more than doubled compared with previous years. Similarly, the Leipzig Authoritarianism Study (Decker et al., 2024) reported renewed increases in xenophobia, chauvinism, and the downplaying of Nazism. In Germany, national reports indicate that extremist offenses have increased year after year (Reuters, 2024). Similar patterns are also observable in the United States and Brazil where not only national surveys reveal an increased openness to military coups and one-party rule (Pew Research Center, 2024a;2024b) but also extremists have attempted to overturn electoral outcomes by storming their respective congresses (European Parliament, 2023).Authoritarian, conservative, and reactionary networks have been among the principal drivers of the surge in distrust, conspiracism, and anti-scientific sentiment (Backhaus et al., 2023;Benegal et al, 2022;Cologna et al., 2025;Dakin et al., 2023;Pavlovic et al., 2022;Philipp-Müller et al., 2022;van Bavel et al, 2022;van der Linden, 2020;Vlasceanu et al., 2024;Voelkel et al., 2024). Coordinated reactionary movements couple conspiratorial and antiscientific narratives as a central element of their political strategy, which, in the recent decades, has been successful in undermining pluralist opponents, galvanizing reactionary followers, shifting blame away from authoritarian sources, and weakening democratic values and institutions (Ren et al., 2022). Through national and international political campaigns, these networks have attempted to replace societal trust and factual knowledge with alternative belief systems that frame them both as victims of ideological attacks and as opponents of a corrupt establishment against the "will of the people" (Golder, 2016;Mudde, 2007;Rothmund et al., 2020;2022;Van Hauwaert et al, 2018;2020). By portraying democracy and science as corrupt, elitist, or ideologically captured establishments, these actors erode public trust in civic society, empirical inquiry, and in the institutions that sustain both (van der Meer et al., 2026). Moreover, in framing pluralist democracy and scientific expertise as nefarious, they reframe their quest for political power and dominance as a project of "renewal" and "social progress".foot_0 This rather paradoxical-and populist (Golder, 2016;Mudde, 2007)-framing has been a potent tool spreading and legitimizing hostility towards pluralist democracy and its knowledge-based institutions. Opposition to democracy, science, and institutional expertise is therefore not an accidental feature of anti-democratic populism and authoritarianism, but its defining component (Capraro et al., 2026;Levitsky and Ziblatt, 2019;Sandel, 2022;Snyder, 2021;Stanley, 2020).These developments illustrate that contemporary polarization does not occur in a vacuum. Rather, it is embedded within a wider ideological shift towards authoritarianism marked by the normalization of violence, systematic dis/misinformation campaigns, and hostility toward scientific expertise (Azevedo and Jost, 2021;Roozenbeek and van der Linden, 2024;van der Linden, 2023;Van Dijck et al., 2018;Vilanova et al., 2025). Against this backdrop, the widespread assumption of the field that polarization is inherently detrimental warrants re-examination. Notably, most empirical studies conceptualize polarization either in ideological terms-typically as increasing divergence or clustering of citizens' attitudes along an ideological spectrum (Philipp-Müller et al., 2022)-or in affective terms, defined as intensified animus toward political outgroups coupled with ingroup favoritism (Iyengar et al., 2012). While analytically tractable, this approach is nonetheless conceptually naïve and methodologically too coarse for the current political moment. Polarization can reflect (i) agonistic democratic conflict-a constitutive feature of pluralist democracy in which adversaries contest values and policies while accepting democratic norms, rules, and outcomes (Mouffe, 2000)-or (ii) democratically corrosive conflict, in which political identity is asymmetrically organized and strategically exploited into distrustful camps and wielded to justify rule-breaking, delegitimizing of elections and pluralist institutions, and erosion of accountability (Somer and McCoy, 2019). Rather than reflecting a symmetric breakdown of civility, this form of dissension constitutes a well-documented, elite-driven pathway to democratic backsliding (Bermeo, 2016). This framing constitutes a tacit belief that social harmony and dialogue are the ideal state of democratic life, and political antagonism is a failure of civility. In the current moment of rising extremism, not all polarization is equivalent nor undesirable. Historical experience underscores this asymmetry. Normative appeals to civility and harmony, when applied indiscriminately, can obscure the moral distinction between domination and resistance-condemning confrontational opposition even in contexts where democratic survival or basic human dignity is at stake. Under conditions of systematic persecution, conflict is not a pathology of democracy but one of the few remaining mechanisms through which injustice can be resisted. Moreover, opposition between democratic and authoritarian values cannot be treated as symmetrical disagreement. When one side rejects pluralism, electoral legitimacy, or epistemic accountability, then 'reconciliation' and even 'civility' function less as neutral civic virtues than as asymmetric constraints, and they risk unilaterally disarming prodemocracy forces. Indeed, democratically corrosive conflict primarily disciplines democratic defenders while leaving anti-democratic actors free to escalate, delegitimize, and capture institutions. Under such asymmetric conditions, appeals to reduce polarization risk equating resistance to authoritarianism with intolerance.The assumption that polarization must be reduced carries profound epistemic and political consequences for social and political psychology and political science. It shapes research designs, variable operationalizations, and the normative interpretations of findings. Interventions aimed at reducing affective polarization, for example, often promote empathy and perspective-taking across ideological divides without considering whether the underlying attitudes sustain or undermine the very conditions that allow for democratic discourse and evidence-based reasoning (e.g., Gillissen et al., 2025;Santos et al., 2022;Tausch et al., 2024;Voelkel et al., 2024;Warner et al., 2020). When authoritarian and anti-scientific movements are gaining strength, such naïve depolarization efforts risk demobilizing both scientific communities and democratic publics at precisely the moment when active defense of evidence and reasoned inquiry is most needed. Safeguarding the norms that make civil society and empirical inquiry possible requires an explicit commitment to democratic rules, pluralism, and epistemic standards (e.g., electoral legitimacy, institutional accountability, and evidence-constrained claims-making). We therefore propose a distinction between democratic polarization-agonistic contestation among actors who accept multicultural pluralism, democratic institutions and election outcomes, civil and human rights, and epistemic accountability-and antidemocratic polarization, in which conflict is strategically mobilized to delegitimize opponents, erode institutional constraints, and normalize dehumanization, scapegoating, misinformation, antiscientific, and conspiratorial narratives as a route to political power. To clarify this distinction, we introduce a minimal framework specifying when political conflict remains within democratic bounds and when it begins to undermine them. This clarification is necessary because much of the polarization literature treats all forms of conflict as analytically comparable, regardless of their implications for democratic functioning.Under contemporary conditions of authoritarian resurgence, disinformation, and institutional erosion, the meaning of democratic conflict cannot be inferred from intensity, incivility, or ideological distance alone. Research treating polarization as presumptively pathological may misclassify democratic resistance as equivalent to anti-democratic escalation. Indeed, democratic politics is not reducible to civility, consensus, or the absence of conflict. In pluralistic societies, conflict is often unavoidable and sometimes necessary. Political disagreement commonly concerns collective priorities, the allocation of public resources, and the design of institutions that structure social life. But democratic disagreement does not extend without limit. It unfolds within a democratic framework that must secure equal standing, basic rights, and meaningful inclusion for all members of the polity. Polarization researchers must contextualize and interpret political conflict in light of the political reality, including stakeholders' commitments to pluralism, equal citizenship, collective welfare, equitable social and economic opportunities, and the maintenance of democratic institutions.This point is especially important because the boundary between "ordinary" policy disagreement and anti-democratic politics cannot be drawn by separating social and economic arrangements from questions of rights, recognition, and membership. Social and economic arrangements are not external to democratic inclusion: they help determine who has security, health, mobility, voice, and genuine access to collective life. When such arrangements systematically expose some groups to deprivation, precarity, premature death, or political marginalization while insulating dominant groups and concentrated economic power, they cease to be ordinary policy disagreements in any normatively innocent sense. They become mechanisms through which hierarchy is organized and reproduced. For this reason, conflicts over rights, recognition, and membership cannot be cleanly separated from conflicts over economic life, since the latter shape whether democratic citizenship is substantive or merely formal. When such inequalities are re-described as routine political disagreement, domination is recast as policy and anti-democratic projects can pass as normal democratic contestation.Accordingly, not all politically salient disagreements are democratically equivalent. Some conflicts occur within democratic bounds, even when they are intense and adversarial. Others target the very conditions that make democratic life possible. When political actors seek to deny full standing to opponents, restrict who counts as a legitimate member of the polity, erode rights protections, weaponize institutions, or degrade the epistemic conditions of public reasoning, the issue is no longer conflict within democracy alone. It is conflict over democracy's own normative and institutional basis. Under such conditions, domination can be redescribed as policy, exclusion can be redescribed as disagreement, and anti-democratic projects can pass as routine democratic contestation.To tentatively point out this distinction, Table 1 provides a heuristic framework for evaluating when political conflict remains consistent with democratic functioning and when it does not. It is not intended as a rigid classificatory scheme, nor does it aim to resolve ambiguities inherent to sociopolitical realities. Rather, it identifies a set of dimensions along which political conflict may be evaluated. The purpose of Table 1 is to make explicit why polarization cannot be evaluated solely in terms of distance, hostility, or civility, as it is often the case in the published literature. What should matter most, in our view, is the nature and kind of conflict being mobilized, by whom, under what institutional conditions, and toward what ends. Contestation over collective priorities, public resources, and institutional arrangements within a framework that protects equal standing, basic rights, and democratic inclusion.Mobilization directed at domination, exclusion, scapegoating, or the denial of full standing, often while being framed as ordinary policy disagreement.The key distinction is not between "material" and "symbolic" conflict, but between conflict within democratic bounds and conflict targeting democracy's normative basic tenets. Treating social and economic arrangements as if they were innocent or neutral background can obscure the existence of democratically corrosive conflict.Opponents are treated as legitimate adversaries within a shared political community, even amid deep disagreement.Opponents are cast as enemies, traitors, contaminants, or groups whose equal standing should be denied.In unequal systems, claims for equal inclusion are often re-described as divisive or extreme.Commitment to fair representation, meaningful participation, and peaceful transfer of power under substantively democratic conditions.Fraud claims without evidence, voter suppression, intimidation, rule manipulation, or other efforts to entrench minority rule.Procedural compliance alone is insufficient. Elections can be formally lawful yet substantively distorted by gerrymandering, disenfranchisement, or unequal access.Rejection of intimidation, vigilantism, and coercive force as tools of routine political competition.Endorsement, normalization, or strategic toleration of violence, threats, or paramilitary intimidation to secure political ends.Defensive resistance under conditions of authoritarian aggression should not be treated as equivalent to aggression in the service of domination.Disagreement proceeds with some commitment to evidence, accountability, policy evaluation, and truth-seeking institutions, even when values differ.Strategic disinformation, conspiracy narratives, epistemic nihilism, anti-scientific attitudes, or the systematic rejection of evidence when it constrains preferred outcomes.Democratic politics requires agreement and commitment to epistemic infrastructure. Accountable institutions for truth-seeking and policy assessment are necessary while not devolving into technocracy nor preaching technocratic infallibility. Preserves equal citizenship, equal protection, minority rights, opposition rights, and inclusive participation as non-negotiable conditions of democratic life.Treats the rights, recognition, safety, or participation of some groups as contingent, revocable, or subordinate to dominantgroup interests.Formal rights may coexist with substantive exclusion. The issue is whether institutions protect equal standing just formally or de facto. A democracy that protects formal liberties while permitting social and economic arrangements that systematically hollow out equal membership remains democratic only in a thin and unstable sense.Supports and materially sustains the institutions that enable accountability, including independent courts, watchdogs, civil society, electoral administration, and a genuinely free press.Defunds, captures, delegitimizes, or selectively weaponizes accountability institutions in order to avoid scrutiny or entrench power.The performative acceptance of democracy is too weak a standard. Democratic conflict must occur while all actors help sustain democratic accountability infrastructures.Clarifies genuine social conflicts, widens participation, increases accountability, and surfaces injustices that require political resolution.Accelerates democratic erosion, normalizes exclusion, immunizes power from scrutiny, and recasts hierarchy as ordinary disagreement.Whether conflict is agonistic depends partly on background power relations, not merely on tone or intensity. For one example, civil and political liberties cannot be exercised meaningfully in the absence of the social and economic conditions that make equal membership, voice, and participation materially possible.This framework indicates that polarization cannot be adequately evaluated solely in terms of attitudinal distance, hostility, or affect. By distinguishing between agonistic and corrosive forms of conflict, it helps account for why comparable levels of polarization may have different implications across contexts. This distinction, however, introduces challenges for classification and measurement. Simpler models may offer greater clarity, parsimony, and cross-context comparability, but these advantages often involve the omission of distinctions that are normatively and politically relevant. As a result, analytically distinct forms of conflict may be treated as equivalent. These limitations are not only methodological but also epistemic, as they relate to how analyses incorporate asymmetries of power, institutional conditions, and the processes through which political meanings and attitudes are formed. with these dimensions and yet their exclusion does not their it may to that are but conceptually in which forms of domination are as ordinary disagreement and anti-democratic is as equivalent to democratic central is not polarization but the of of polarization research to conflict as presumptively pathological while distinguishing democratically contestation from democratically corrosive under conditions of and epistemic it is not that efforts toward depolarization are inherently nor that all research on polarization treats polarization as undesirable.
